I swear to thee, youth, by the white hand of Rosalind,
I am that he, that unfortunate he.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: But are you so much in love as your rhymes speak?

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: Neither rhyme nor reason can express how much.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Love is merely a madness and, I tell you, deserves as
well a dark house and a whip as madmen do, and the
reason why they are not so punished and cured is that
the lunacy is so ordinary that the whippers are in love,
too. Yet I profess curing it by counsel.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: Did you ever cure any so?

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Yes, one, and in this manner. He was to imagine me his
love, his mistress, and I set him every day to woo me;
at which time would I, being but a moonish youth,
grieve, be effeminate, changeable, longing and liking,
proud, fantastical, apish, shallow, inconstant, full of
tears, full of smiles; for every passion something, and
for no passion truly anything, as boys and women are,
for the most part, cattle of this color; would now like
him, now loathe him; then entertain him, then forswear
him; now weep for him, then spit at him, that I drave my
suitor from his mad humor of love to a living humor of
madness, which was to forswear the full stream of the
world and to live in a nook merely monastic. And thus I
cured him, and this way will I take upon me to wash your
liver as clean as a sound sheep’s heart, that there
shall not be one spot of love in ’t.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: I would not be cured, youth.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
I would cure you if you would but call me Rosalind and
come every day to my cote and woo me.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text:
Now, by the faith of my love, I will. Tell me where it
is.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Go with me to it, and I’ll show it you; and by the way
you shall tell me where in the forest you live. Will
you go?

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: With all my heart, good youth.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Nay, you must call me Rosalind.—Come, sister, will you
go?
Exeunt
Enter TOUCHSTONE and AUDREY, and JAQUES behind

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
Come apace, good Audrey. I will fetch up your goats,
Audrey. And how, Audrey? Am I the man yet? Doth my
simple feature content you?

units.speaker: AUDREY
units.text: Your features, Lord warrant us! What features?

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
I am here with thee and thy goats, as the most
capricious poet, honest Ovid, was among the Goths.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text:
[aside] O knowledge ill-inhabited, worse than Jove in a
thatched house.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
When a man’s verses cannot be understood nor a man’s
good wit seconded with the forward child, understanding,
it strikes a man more dead than a great reckoning in a
little room. Truly, I would the gods had made thee
poetical.

units.speaker: AUDREY
units.text:
I do not know what “poetical” is. Is it honest in deed
and word? Is it a true thing?

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
No, truly, for the truest poetry is the most feigning,
and lovers are given to poetry, and what they swear in
poetry may be said as lovers they do feign.

units.speaker: AUDREY
units.text: Do you wish then that the gods had made me poetical?

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
I do, truly, for thou swear’st to me thou art honest.
Now, if thou wert a poet, I might have some hope thou
didst feign.

units.speaker: AUDREY
units.text: Would you not have me honest?

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
No, truly, unless thou wert hard-favored, for honesty
coupled to beauty is to have honey a sauce to sugar.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text: [aside] A material fool.

units.speaker: AUDREY
units.text:
Well, I am not fair, and therefore I pray the gods make
me honest.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
Truly, and to cast away honesty upon a foul slut were
to put good meat into an unclean dish.

units.speaker: AUDREY
units.text: I am not a slut, though I thank the gods I am foul.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
Well, praised be the gods for thy foulness;
sluttishness may come hereafter. But be it as it may be,
I will marry thee; and to that end I have been with Sir
Oliver Martext, the vicar of the next village, who hath
promised to meet me in this place of the forest and to
couple us.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text: [aside] I would fain see this meeting.

units.speaker: AUDREY
units.text: Well, the gods give us joy.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
Amen. A man may, if he were of a fearful heart, stagger
in this attempt, for here we have no temple but the
wood, no assembly but horn-beasts. But what though?
Courage. As horns are odious, they are necessary. It is
said, “Many a man knows no end of his goods.” Right:
many a man has good horns and knows no end of
them. Well, that is the dowry of his wife; ’tis none of
his own getting. Horns? Even so. Poor men alone? No, no.
The noblest deer hath them as huge as the rascal. Is
the single man therefore blessed? No. As a walled town
is more worthier than a village, so is the forehead of a
married man more honorable than the bare brow of a
bachelor. And by how much defense is better than no
skill, by so much is a horn more precious than to want.

units.speaker: SIR OLIVER MARTEXT
units.text: Is there none here to give the woman?

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text: I will not take her on gift of any man.

units.speaker: SIR OLIVER MARTEXT
units.text:
Truly, she must be given, or the marriage is not
lawful.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text: [advancing] Proceed, proceed. I’ll give her.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
Good even, good Monsieur What-ye-call’t. How do you,
sir? You are very well met. God 'ild you for your last
company. I am very glad to see you. Even a toy in hand
here, sir. Nay, pray be covered.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text: Will you be married, motley?

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
As the ox hath his bow, sir, the horse his curb, and
the falcon her bells, so man hath his desires; and as
pigeons bill, so wedlock would be nibbling.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text:
And will you, being a man of your breeding, be married
under a bush like a beggar? Get you to church, and have
a good priest that can tell you what marriage is. This
fellow will but join you together as they join wainscot.
Then one of you will prove a shrunk panel and, like
green timber, warp, warp.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
[aside] I am not in the mind but I were better to be
married of him than of another, for he is not like to
marry me well, and not being well married, it will be a
good excuse for me hereafter to leave my wife.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text: Go thou with me, and let me counsel thee.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
Come, sweet Audrey.
We must be married, or we must live in bawdry.—
Farewell, good Master Oliver, not
O sweet Oliver,
O brave Oliver,
Leave me not behind thee
But
Wind away,
Begone, I say,
I will not to wedding with thee.
Exeunt JAQUES, TOUCHSTONE, and AUDREY

units.speaker: SIR OLIVER MARTEXT
units.text:
'Tis no matter. Ne'er a fantastical knave of them all
shall flout me out of my calling.
Exit
Enter ROSALIND and CELIA

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: Never talk to me. I will weep.

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text:
Do, I prithee, but yet have the grace to consider that
tears do not become a man.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: But have I not cause to weep?

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text: As good cause as one would desire. Therefore weep.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: His very hair is of the dissembling color.

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text:
Something browner than Judas’s. Marry, his kisses are
Judas’s own children.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: I' faith, his hair is of a good color.

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text:
An excellent color. Your chestnut was ever the only
color.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
And his kissing is as full of sanctity as the touch of
holy bread.

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text:
He hath bought a pair of cast lips of Diana. A nun of
winter’s sisterhood kisses not more religiously. The
very ice of chastity is in them.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
But why did he swear he would come this morning, and
comes not?

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text: Nay, certainly, there is no truth in him.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: Do you think so?

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text:
Yes, I think he is not a pick-purse nor a
horse-stealer, but for his verity in love, I do think
him as concave as a covered goblet or a worm-eaten nut.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: Not true in love?

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text: Yes, when he is in, but I think he is not in.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: You have heard him swear downright he was.

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text:
“Was” is not “is.” Besides, the oath of a lover is no
stronger than the word of a tapster. They are both the
confirmer of false reckonings. He attends here in the
forest on the duke your father.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
I met the duke yesterday and had much question with
him. He asked me of what parentage I was. I told him, of
as good as he. So he laughed and let me go. But what
talk we of fathers when there is such a man as Orlando?

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text:
Oh, that’s a brave man. He writes brave verses, speaks
brave words, swears brave oaths, and breaks them
bravely, quite traverse, athwart the heart of his lover,
as a puny tilter that spurs his horse but on one side
breaks his staff like a noble goose; but all’s brave
that youth mounts and folly guides.
Enter CORIN

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text: Who comes here?

units.speaker: CORIN
units.text:
Mistress and master, you have oft inquired
After the shepherd that complained of love,
Who you saw sitting by me on the turf,
Praising the proud disdainful shepherdess
That was his mistress.

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text: [As Aliena] Well, and what of him?

units.speaker: CORIN
units.text:
If you will see a pageant truly played
Between the pale complexion of true love
And the red glow of scorn and proud disdain,
Go hence a little, and I shall conduct you,
If you will mark it.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
[aside to CELIA] O, come, let us remove.
The sight of lovers feedeth those in love.
[as Ganymede, to CORIN] Bring us to this sight, and you
shall say
I’ll prove a busy actor in their play.
Exeunt
Enter SILVIUS and PHOEBE

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text:
Sweet Phoebe, do not scorn me. Do not, Phoebe.
Say that you love me not, but say not so
In bitterness. The common executioner,
Whose heart th' accustomed sight of death makes hard,
Falls not the axe upon the humbled neck
But first begs pardon. Will you sterner be
Than he that dies and lives by bloody drops?
Enter ROSALIND, CELIA, and CORIN, behind

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text:
I would not be thy executioner.
I fly thee, for I would not injure thee.
Thou tell’st me there is murder in mine eye.
'Tis pretty, sure, and very probable
That eyes, that are the frail’st and softest things,
Who shut their coward gates on atomies,
Should be called tyrants, butchers, murderers.
Now I do frown on thee with all my heart,
And if mine eyes can wound, now let them kill thee.
Now counterfeit to swoon, why, now fall down;
Or if thou canst not, Oh, for shame, for shame,
Lie not, to say mine eyes are murderers.
Now show the wound mine eye hath made in thee.
Scratch thee but with a pin, and there remains
Some scar of it. Lean upon a rush,
The cicatrice and capable impressure
Thy palm some moment keeps. But now mine eyes,
Which I have darted at thee, hurt thee not.
Nor, I am sure, there is no force in eyes
That can do hurt.

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text:
O dear Phoebe,
If ever—as that ever may be near—
You meet in some fresh cheek the power of fancy,
Then shall you know the wounds invisible
That love’s keen arrows make.

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text:
But till that time
Come not thou near me. And when that time comes,
Afflict me with thy mocks, pity me not,
As till that time I shall not pity thee.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
[Advancing, as Ganymede] And why, I pray you? Who might
be your mother,
That you insult, exult, and all at once,
Over the wretched? What though you have no beauty—
As, by my faith, I see no more in you
Than without candle may go dark to bed—
Must you be therefore proud and pitiless?
Why, what means this? Why do you look on me?
I see no more in you than in the ordinary
Of nature’s sale-work.— 'Od’s my little life,
I think she means to tangle my eyes, too.
—No, faith, proud mistress, hope not after it.
'Tis not your inky brows, your black silk hair,
Your bugle eyeballs, nor your cheek of cream
That can entame my spirits to your worship.
—You foolish shepherd, wherefore do you follow her,
Like foggy south puffing with wind and rain?
You are a thousand times a properer man
Than she a woman. 'Tis such fools as you
That makes the world full of ill-favored children.
'Tis not her glass but you that flatters her,
And out of you she sees herself more proper
Than any of her lineaments can show her.
—But, mistress, know yourself. Down on your knees
And thank heaven, fasting, for a good man’s love,
For I must tell you friendly in your ear,
Sell when you can; you are not for all markets.
Cry the man mercy, love him, take his offer.
Foul is most foul, being foul to be a scoffer.
—So take her to thee, shepherd. Fare you well.

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text:
Sweet youth, I pray you chide a year together.
I had rather hear you chide than this man woo.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
He’s fall'n in love with your foulness. [to SILVIUS]
And she’ll fall in love with my anger. If it be so, as
fast as she answers thee with frowning looks, I’ll sauce
her with bitter words. [to PHOEBE] Why look you so upon
me?

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text: For no ill will I bear you.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
I pray you, do not fall in love with me,
For I am falser than vows made in wine.
Besides, I like you not. If you will know my house,
'Tis at the tuft of olives, here hard by.
—Will you go, sister?— Shepherd, ply her hard.
—Come, sister.— Shepherdess, look on him better,
And be not proud. Though all the world could see,
None could be so abused in sight as he.
—Come, to our flock.
Exeunt ROSALIND, CELIA and CORIN

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text:
Dead shepherd, now I find thy saw of might:
“Who ever loved that loved not at first sight?”

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text: Sweet Phoebe—

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text: Ha, what sayst thou, Silvius?

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text: Sweet Phoebe, pity me.

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text: Why, I am sorry for thee, gentle Silvius.

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text:
Wherever sorrow is, relief would be.
If you do sorrow at my grief in love,
By giving love your sorrow and my grief
Were both extermined.

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text: Thou hast my love. Is not that neighborly?

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text: I would have you.

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text:
Why, that were covetousness.
Silvius, the time was that I hated thee,
And yet it is not that I bear thee love,
But since that thou canst talk of love so well,
Thy company, which erst was irksome to me,
I will endure, and I’ll employ thee too.
But do not look for further recompense
Than thine own gladness that thou art employed.

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text:
So holy and so perfect is my love,
And I in such a poverty of grace,
That I shall think it a most plenteous crop
To glean the broken ears after the man
That the main harvest reaps. Loose now and then
A scattered smile, and that I’ll live upon.

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text: Know’st thou the youth that spoke to me erewhile?

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text:
Not very well, but I have met him oft,
And he hath bought the cottage and the bounds
That the old carlot once was master of.

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text:
Think not I love him, though I ask for him.
'Tis but a peevish boy—yet he talks well—
But what care I for words? Yet words do well
When he that speaks them pleases those that hear.
It is a pretty youth—not very pretty—
But sure he’s proud—and yet his pride becomes him.
He’ll make a proper man. The best thing in him
Is his complexion; and faster than his tongue
Did make offense, his eye did heal it up.
He is not very tall—yet for his years he’s tall.
His leg is but so-so—and yet ’tis well.
There was a pretty redness in his lip,
A little riper and more lusty red
Than that mixed in his cheek: ’twas just the difference
Betwixt the constant red and mingled damask.
There be some women, Silvius, had they marked him
In parcels as I did, would have gone near
To fall in love with him; but for my part
I love him not nor hate him not; and yet
I have more cause to hate him than to love him.
For what had he to do to chide at me?
He said mine eyes were black and my hair black
And, now I am remembered, scorned at me.
I marvel why I answered not again.
But that’s all one: omittance is no quittance.
I’ll write to him a very taunting letter,
And thou shalt bear it. Wilt thou, Silvius?

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text: Phoebe, with all my heart.

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text:
I’ll write it straight.
The matter’s in my head and in my heart.
I will be bitter with him and passing short.
Go with me, Silvius.
Exeunt
Enter ROSALIND, CELIA, and JAQUES

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text:
I prithee, pretty youth, let me be better acquainted
with thee.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: They say you are a melancholy fellow.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text: I am so. I do love it better than laughing.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Those that are in extremity of either are abominable
fellows and betray themselves to every modern censure
worse than drunkards.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text: Why, ’tis good to be sad and say nothing.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: Why then, ’tis good to be a post.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text:
I have neither the scholar’s melancholy, which is
emulation; nor the musician’s, which is fantastical; nor
the courtier’s, which is proud; nor the soldier’s,
which is ambitious; nor the lawyer’s, which is politic;
nor the lady’s, which is nice; nor the lover’s, which is
all these, but it is a melancholy of mine own,
compounded of many simples, extracted from many objects,
and indeed the sundry contemplation of my travels, in
which my often rumination wraps me in a most humorous
sadness.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
A traveler. By my faith, you have great reason to be
sad. I fear you have sold your own lands to see other
men’s. Then to have seen much and to have nothing is to
have rich eyes and poor hands.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text: Yes, I have gained my experience.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
And your experience makes you sad. I had rather have a
fool to make me merry than experience to make me sad—and
to travel for it, too.
Enter ORLANDO

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: Good day and happiness, dear Rosalind.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text: Nay then, God be wi' you, an you talk in blank verse.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Farewell, Monsieur Traveler. Look you lisp and wear
strange suits, disable all the benefits of your own
country, be out of love with your nativity, and almost
chide God for making you that countenance you are, or I
will scarce think you have swam in a gondola.
Exit JAQUES

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
[as Ganymede pretending to be ROSALIND] Why, how now,
Orlando, where have you been all this while? You a
lover? An you serve me such another trick, never come in
my sight more.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: My fair Rosalind, I come within an hour of my promise.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Break an hour’s promise in love? He that will divide a
minute into a thousand parts and break but a part of the
thousand part of a minute in the affairs of love, it
may be said of him that Cupid hath clapped him o' th'
shoulder, but I’ll warrant him heart-whole.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: Pardon me, dear Rosalind.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Nay, an you be so tardy, come no more in my sight. I
had as lief be wooed of a snail.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: Of a snail?

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Ay, of a snail, for though he comes slowly, he carries
his house on his head—a better jointure, I think, than
you make a woman. Besides, he brings his destiny with
him.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: What’s that?

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Why, horns, which such as you are fain to be beholding
to your wives for. But he comes armed in his fortune and
prevents the slander of his wife.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: Virtue is no hornmaker, and my Rosalind is virtuous.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: And I am your Rosalind.

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text:
[as Aliena] It pleases him to call you so, but he hath
a
Rosalind of a better leer than you.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Come, woo me, woo me, for now I am in a holiday humor,
and like enough to consent. What would you say to me
now, an I were your very, very Rosalind?

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: I would kiss before I spoke.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Nay, you were better speak first, and when you were
graveled for lack of matter, you might take occasion to
kiss. Very good orators, when they are out, they will
spit; and for lovers lacking—God warn us—matter, the
cleanliest shift is to kiss.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: How if the kiss be denied?

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Then she puts you to entreaty, and there begins new
matter.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: Who could be out, being before his beloved mistress?

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Marry, that should you if I were your mistress, or I
should think my honesty ranker than my wit.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: What, of my suit?

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Not out of your apparel, and yet out of your suit. Am
not I your Rosalind?

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text:
I take some joy to say you are because I would be
talking of her.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: Well, in her person I say I will not have you.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: Then, in mine own person I die.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
No, faith, die by attorney. The poor world is almost
six thousand years old, and in all this time there was
not any man died in his own person, videlicet, in a love
cause. Troilus had his brains dashed out with a Grecian
club, yet he did what he could to die before, and he is
one of the patterns of love. Leander, he would have
lived many a fair year though Hero had turned nun if it
had not been for a hot midsummer night, for, good youth,
he went but forth to wash him in the Hellespont and,
being taken with the cramp, was drowned; and the foolish
chroniclers of that age found it was Hero of Sestos.
But these are all lies. Men have died from time to time,
and worms have eaten them, but not for love.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text:
I would not have my right Rosalind of this mind, for I
protest her frown might kill me.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
By this hand, it will not kill a fly. But come; now I
will be your Rosalind in a more coming-on disposition,
and ask me what you will, I will grant it.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: Then love me, Rosalind.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: Yes, faith, will I, Fridays and Saturdays and all.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: And wilt thou have me?

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: Ay, and twenty such.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: What sayest thou?

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: Are you not good?

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: I hope so.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Why then, can one desire too much of a good thing?—
Come, sister, you shall be the priest and marry us.—Give
me your hand, Orlando.—What do you say, sister?

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: Pray thee, marry us.

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text: I cannot say the words.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: You must begin “Will you, Orlando—”

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text: Go to.—Will you, Orlando, have to wife this Rosalind?

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: I will.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: Ay, but when?

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: Why, now, as fast as she can marry us.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: Then you must say “I take thee, Rosalind, for wife.”

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: I take thee, Rosalind, for wife.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
I might ask you for your commission, but I do take
thee, Orlando, for my husband. There’s a girl goes
before the priest, and certainly a woman’s thought runs
before her actions.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: So do all thoughts. They are winged.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Now tell me how long you would have her after you have
possessed her.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: Forever and a day.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Say “a day” without the “ever.” No, no, Orlando, men
are April when they woo, December when they wed. Maids
are May when they are maids, but the sky changes when
they are wives. I will be more jealous of thee than a
Barbary cock- pigeon over his hen, more clamorous than a
parrot against rain, more newfangled than an ape, more
giddy in my desires than a monkey. I will weep for
nothing, like Diana in the fountain, and I will do that
when you are disposed to be merry. I will laugh like a
hyena, and that when thou art inclined to sleep.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: But will my Rosalind do so?

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: By my life, she will do as I do.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: Oh, but she is wise.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Or else she could not have the wit to do this. The
wiser, the waywarder. Make the doors upon a woman’s wit,
and it will out at the casement. Shut that, and ’twill
out at the keyhole. Stop that, ’twill fly with the smoke
out at the chimney.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text:
A man that had a wife with such a wit, he might say
“Wit, whither wilt?”

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Nay, you might keep that check for it, till you met
your wife’s wit going to your neighbor’s bed.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: And what wit could wit have to excuse that?

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Marry, to say she came to seek you there. You shall
never take her without her answer unless you take her
without her tongue. Oh, that woman that cannot make her
fault her husband’s occasion, let her never nurse her
child herself, for she will breed it like a fool.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: For these two hours, Rosalind, I will leave thee.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: Alas, dear love, I cannot lack thee two hours.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text:
I must attend the duke at dinner. By two o'clock I will
be with thee again.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Ay, go your ways, go your ways. I knew what you would
prove. My friends told me as much, and I thought no
less. That flattering tongue of yours won me. 'Tis but
one cast away, and so, come, death. Two o'clock is your
hour?

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: Ay, sweet Rosalind.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
By my troth, and in good earnest, and so God mend me,
and by all pretty oaths that are not dangerous, if you
break one jot of your promise or come one minute behind
your hour, I will think you the most pathetical
break-promise and the most hollow lover and the most
unworthy of her you call Rosalind that may be chosen out
of the gross band of the unfaithful. Therefore beware
my censure, and keep your promise.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text:
With no less religion than if thou wert indeed my
Rosalind.
So, adieu.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Well, time is the old justice that examines all such
offenders, and let time try. Adieu.
Exit ORLANDO

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text:
You have simply misused our sex in your love-prate. We
must have your doublet and hose plucked over your head
and show the world what the bird hath done to her own
nest.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
O coz, coz, coz, my pretty little coz, that thou didst
know how many fathom deep I am in love. But it cannot
be sounded; my affection hath an unknown bottom, like
the Bay of Portugal.

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text:
Or rather bottomless, that as fast as you pour
affection in, it runs out.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
No, that same wicked bastard of Venus that was begot of
thought, conceived of spleen, and born of madness, that
blind rascally boy that abuses everyone’s eyes because
his own are out, let him be judge how deep I am in love.
I’ll tell thee, Aliena, I cannot be out of the sight of
Orlando. I’ll go find a shadow and sigh till he come.

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text: And I’ll sleep.
Exeunt
Enter JAQUES and LORDS, like foresters

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text: Which is he that killed the deer?

units.speaker: FIRST LORD
units.text: Sir, it was I.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text:
Let’s present him to the duke like a Roman conqueror.
And it would do well to set the deer’s horns upon his
head for a branch of victory.—Have you no song,
forester, for this purpose?

units.speaker: SECOND LORD
units.text: Yes, sir.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text:
Sing it. 'Tis no matter how it be in tune, so it make
noise enough.

units.speaker: SECOND LORD
units.text:
[sings]
What shall he have that killed the deer?
His leather skin and horns to wear.
Then sing him home.
(The rest shall bear this burden.)
Take thou no scorn to wear the horn.
It was a crest ere thou wast born.
Thy father’s father wore it,
And thy father bore it.
The horn, the horn, the lusty horn
Is not a thing to laugh to scorn.
Exeunt
Enter ROSALIND and CELIA

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
How say you now? Is it not past two o'clock? And here
much Orlando.

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text:
I warrant you, with pure love and troubled brain he
hath ta'en his bow and arrows and is gone forth to
sleep.
Enter SILVIUS

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text: Look who comes here.

[To ROSALIND] My errand is to you, fair youth.
My gentle Phoebe did bid me give you this.
I know not the contents, but as I guess
By the stern brow and waspish action
Which she did use as she was writing of it,
It bears an angry tenor. Pardon me.
I am but as a guiltless messenger.
[Gives the letter]

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
[Examines the letter as Ganymede] Patience herself
would startle at this letter
And play the swaggerer. Bear this, bear all.
She says I am not fair, that I lack manners.
She calls me proud, and that she could not love me
Were man as rare as phoenix. 'Od’s my will,
Her love is not the hare that I do hunt.
Why writes she so to me? Well, shepherd, well,
This is a letter of your own device.

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text:
No, I protest, I know not the contents.
Phoebe did write it.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Come, come, you are a fool,
And turned into the extremity of love.
I saw her hand. She has a leathern hand,
A freestone-colored hand. I verily did think
That her old gloves were on, but ’twas her hands.
She has a huswife’s hand—but that’s no matter.
I say she never did invent this letter.
This is a man’s invention, and his hand.

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text: Sure it is hers.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Why, ’tis a boisterous and a cruel style,
A style for challengers. Why, she defies me
Like Turk to Christian. Women’s gentle brain
Could not drop forth such giant-rude invention,
Such Ethiop words, blacker in their effect
Than in their countenance. Will you hear the letter?

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text:
So please you, for I never heard it yet,
Yet heard too much of Phoebe’s cruelty.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
She Phoebes me. Mark how the tyrant writes.
[reads] Art thou god to shepherd turned,
That a maiden’s heart hath burned?
Can a woman rail thus?

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text: Call you this railing?

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
[reads]
Why, thy godhead laid apart,
Warr’st thou with a woman’s heart?
Did you ever hear such railing?
Whiles the eye of man did woo me,
That could do no vengeance to me.
Meaning me a beast.
If the scorn of your bright eyne
Have power to raise such love in mine,
Alack, in me what strange effect
Would they work in mild aspect?
Whiles you chid me, I did love.
How then might your prayers move?
He that brings this love to thee
Little knows this love in me,
And by him seal up thy mind
Whether that thy youth and kind
Will the faithful offer take
Of me, and all that I can make,
Or else by him my love deny,
And then I’ll study how to die.

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text: Call you this chiding?

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text: [As Aliena] Alas, poor shepherd.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Do you pity him? No, he deserves no pity.— Wilt thou
love such a woman? What, to make thee an instrument and
play false strains upon thee? Not to be endured. Well,
go your way to her, for I see love hath made thee a tame
snake, and say this to her: that if she love me, I
charge her to love thee; if she will not, I will never
have her unless thou entreat for her. If you be a true
lover, hence and not a word, for here comes more
company.
Exit SILVIUS
Enter OLIVER

units.speaker: OLIVER
units.text:
Good morrow, fair ones. Pray you, if you know,
Where in the purlieus of this forest stands
A sheepcote fenced about with olive trees?

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text:
[as Aliena] West of this place, down in the neighbor
bottom,
The rank of osiers by the murmuring stream
Left on your right hand brings you to the place.
But at this hour the house doth keep itself.
There’s none within.

units.speaker: OLIVER
units.text:
If that an eye may profit by a tongue,
Then should I know you by description.
Such garments, and such years. “The boy is fair,
Of female favor, and bestows himself
Like a ripe sister; the woman low
And browner than her brother.” Are not you
The owner of the house I did inquire for?

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text: It is no boast, being asked, to say we are.

units.speaker: OLIVER
units.text:
Orlando doth commend him to you both,
And to that youth he calls his Rosalind
He sends this bloody napkin. Are you he?

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: [as Ganymede] I am. What must we understand by this?

units.speaker: OLIVER
units.text:
Some of my shame, if you will know of me
What man I am, and how, and why, and where
This handkercher was stained.

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text: I pray you, tell it.

units.speaker: OLIVER
units.text:
When last the young Orlando parted from you,
He left a promise to return again
Within an hour, and pacing through the forest,
Chewing the food of sweet and bitter fancy,
Lo, what befell. He threw his eye aside—
And mark what object did present itself:
Under an old oak, whose boughs were mossed with age
And high top bald with dry antiquity,
A wretched, ragged man, o'ergrown with hair,
Lay sleeping on his back. About his neck
A green and gilded snake had wreathed itself,
Who with her head, nimble in threats, approached
The opening of his mouth. But suddenly,
Seeing Orlando, it unlinked itself
And, with indented glides, did slip away
Into a bush, under which bush’s shade
A lioness, with udders all drawn dry,
Lay couching, head on ground, with catlike watch
When that the sleeping man should stir—for ’tis
The royal disposition of that beast
To prey on nothing that doth seem as dead.
This seen, Orlando did approach the man
And found it was his brother, his elder brother.

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text:
Oh, I have heard him speak of that same brother,
And he did render him the most unnatural
That lived amongst men.

units.speaker: OLIVER
units.text:
And well he might so do,
For well I know he was unnatural.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
But to Orlando: did he leave him there,
Food to the sucked and hungry lioness?

units.speaker: OLIVER
units.text:
Twice did he turn his back and purposed so,
But kindness, nobler ever than revenge,
And nature, stronger than his just occasion,
Made him give battle to the lioness,
Who quickly fell before him; in which hurtling,
From miserable slumber I awaked.

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text: Are you his brother?

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: Was ’t you he rescued?

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text: Was ’t you that did so oft contrive to kill him?

units.speaker: OLIVER
units.text:
'Twas I, but ’tis not I. I do not shame
To tell you what I was, since my conversion
So sweetly tastes, being the thing I am.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: But for the bloody napkin?

units.speaker: OLIVER
units.text:
By and by.
When from the first to last betwixt us two
Tears our recountments had most kindly bathed—
As how I came into that desert place—
In brief, he led me to the gentle duke,
Who gave me fresh array and entertainment,
Committing me unto my brother’s love,
Who led me instantly unto his cave,
There stripped himself, and here upon his arm
The lioness had torn some flesh away,
Which all this while had bled. And now he fainted,
And cried in fainting upon Rosalind.
Brief, I recovered him, bound up his wound,
And after some small space, being strong at heart,
He sent me hither, stranger as I am,
To tell this story, that you might excuse
His broken promise, and to give this napkin
Dyed in his blood unto the shepherd youth
That he in sport doth call his Rosalind.

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text: Why, how now, Ganymede, sweet Ganymede?

units.speaker: OLIVER
units.text: Many will swoon when they do look on blood.

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text: There is more in it.—Cousin Ganymede.

units.speaker: OLIVER
units.text: Look, he recovers.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: I would I were at home.

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text:
We’ll lead you thither.
—I pray you, will you take him by the arm?

units.speaker: OLIVER
units.text:
Be of good cheer, youth. You a man? You lack a man’s
heart.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
I do so, I confess it. Ah, sirrah, a body would think
this was well-counterfeited. I pray you tell your
brother how well I counterfeited. Heigh-ho.

units.speaker: OLIVER
units.text:
This was not counterfeit. There is too great testimony
in your complexion that it was a passion of earnest.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: Counterfeit, I assure you.

units.speaker: OLIVER
units.text:
Well then, take a good heart and counterfeit to be a
man.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
So I do. But i' faith, I should have been a woman by
right.

units.speaker: CELIA
units.text:
Come, you look paler and paler. Pray you, draw
homewards.—Good sir, go with us.

units.speaker: OLIVER
units.text:
That will I, for I must bear answer back
How you excuse my brother, Rosalind.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
I shall devise something. But I pray you commend my
counterfeiting to him. Will you go?
Exeunt
Enter TOUCHSTONE and AUDREY

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text: We shall find a time, Audrey. Patience, gentle Audrey.

units.speaker: AUDREY
units.text:
Faith, the priest was good enough, for all the old
gentleman’s saying.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
A most wicked Sir Oliver, Audrey, a most vile Martext.
But, Audrey, there is a youth here in the forest lays
claim to you.

units.speaker: AUDREY
units.text:
Ay, I know who ’tis. He hath no interest in me in the
world.
Enter WILLIAM

units.speaker: AUDREY
units.text: Here comes the man you mean.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
It is meat and drink to me to see a clown. By my
troth, we that have good wits have much to answer for.
We shall be flouting. We cannot hold.

units.speaker: WILLIAM
units.text: Good ev'n, Audrey.

units.speaker: AUDREY
units.text: God gi' good ev'n, William.

units.speaker: WILLIAM
units.text: And good ev'n to you, sir.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
Good ev'n, gentle friend. Cover thy head, cover thy
head.
Nay, prithee, be covered. How old are you, friend?

units.speaker: WILLIAM
units.text: Five-and-twenty, sir.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text: A ripe age. Is thy name William?

units.speaker: WILLIAM
units.text: William, sir.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text: A fair name. Wast born i' th' forest here?

units.speaker: WILLIAM
units.text: Ay, sir, I thank God.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text: “Thank God.” A good answer. Art rich?

units.speaker: WILLIAM
units.text: 'Faith, sir, so-so.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
“So-so” is good, very good, very excellent good. And
yet it is not: it is but so-so. Art thou wise?

units.speaker: WILLIAM
units.text: Ay, sir, I have a pretty wit.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
Why, thou sayst well. I do now remember a saying: “The
fool doth think he is wise, but the wise man knows
himself to be a fool.” The heathen philosopher, when he
had a desire to eat a grape, would open his lips when he
put it into his mouth, meaning thereby that grapes were
made to eat and lips to open. You do love this maid?

units.speaker: WILLIAM
units.text: I do, sir.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text: Give me your hand. Art thou learned?

units.speaker: WILLIAM
units.text: No, sir.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
Then learn this of me: to have is to have. For it is a
figure in rhetoric that drink, being poured out of a cup
into a glass, by filling the one doth empty the other.
For all your writers do consent that ipse is “he.” Now,
you are not ipse, for I am he.

units.speaker: WILLIAM
units.text: Which he, sir?

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
He, sir, that must marry this woman. Therefore, you
clown, abandon—which is, in the vulgar, “leave”—the
society—which in the boorish is “company”—of this
female—which in the common is “woman” ; which together
is, abandon the society of this female, or, clown, thou
perishest; or, to thy better understanding, diest; or,
to wit, I kill thee, make thee away, translate thy life
into death, thy liberty into bondage. I will deal in
poison with thee, or in bastinado, or in steel. I will
bandy with thee in faction. I will o'errun thee with
policy. I will kill thee a hundred and fifty ways.
Therefore tremble and depart.

units.speaker: AUDREY
units.text: Do, good William.

units.speaker: WILLIAM
units.text: God rest you merry, sir.
Exit
Enter CORIN

units.speaker: CORIN
units.text: Our master and mistress seeks you. Come away, away.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text: Trip, Audrey, trip, Audrey.—I attend, I attend.
Exeunt
Enter ORLANDO and OLIVER

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text:
Is ’t possible that on so little acquaintance you
should like her? That, but seeing, you should love her?
And loving, woo? And wooing, she should grant? And will
you persevere to enjoy her?

units.speaker: OLIVER
units.text:
Neither call the giddiness of it in question, the
poverty of her, the small acquaintance, my sudden
wooing, nor her sudden consenting, but say with me “I
love Aliena”; say with her that she loves me; consent
with both that we may enjoy each other. It shall be to
your good, for my father’s house and all the revenue
that was old Sir Rowland’s will I estate upon you, and
here live and die a shepherd.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text:
You have my consent. Let your wedding be tomorrow.
Thither will I invite the duke and all’s contented
followers.
Go you and prepare Aliena, for look you, here comes my
Rosalind.
Enter ROSALIND

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: [As Ganymede] God save you, brother.

units.speaker: OLIVER
units.text: And you, fair sister.
Exit

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
O my dear Orlando, how it grieves me to see thee wear
thy heart in a scarf.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: It is my arm.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
I thought thy heart had been wounded with the claws of
a lion.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: Wounded it is, but with the eyes of a lady.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Did your brother tell you how I counterfeited to swoon
when he showed me your handkercher?

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: Ay, and greater wonders than that.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Oh, I know where you are. Nay, ’tis true. There was
never anything so sudden but the fight of two rams and
Caesar’s thrasonical brag of “I came, saw, and
overcame.” For your brother and my sister no sooner met
but they looked, no sooner looked but they loved, no
sooner loved but they sighed, no sooner sighed but they
asked one another the reason, no sooner knew the reason
but they sought the remedy; and in these degrees have
they made a pair of stairs to marriage, which they will
climb incontinent, or else be incontinent before
marriage. They are in the very wrath of love, and they
will together. Clubs cannot part them.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text:
They shall be married tomorrow, and I will bid the duke
to the nuptial. But Oh, how bitter a thing it is to
look into happiness through another man’s eyes. By so
much the more shall I tomorrow be at the height of
heart-heaviness, by how much I shall think my brother
happy in having what he wishes for.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Why, then, tomorrow I cannot serve your turn for
Rosalind?

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: I can live no longer by thinking.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
I will weary you then no longer with idle talking. Know
of me then—for now I speak to some purpose—that I know
you are a gentleman of good conceit. I speak not this
that you should bear a good opinion of my knowledge,
insomuch I say I know you are. Neither do I labor for a
greater esteem than may in some little measure draw a
belief from you to do yourself good, and not to grace
me. Believe then, if you please, that I can do strange
things. I have, since I was three year old, conversed
with a magician, most profound in his art and yet not
damnable. If you do love Rosalind so near the heart as
your gesture cries it out, when your brother marries
Aliena shall you marry her. I know into what straits of
fortune she is driven, and it is not impossible to me,
if it appear not inconvenient to you, to set her before
your eyes tomorrow, human as she is, and without any
danger.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: Speak’st thou in sober meanings?

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
By my life I do, which I tender dearly, though I say I
am a magician. Therefore put you in your best array, bid
your friends; for if you will be married tomorrow, you
shall, and to Rosalind, if you will.
Enter SILVIUS and PHOEBE

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: Look, here comes a lover of mine and a lover of hers.

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text:
Youth, you have done me much ungentleness
To show the letter that I writ to you.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
I care not if I have. It is my study
To seem despiteful and ungentle to you.
You are there followed by a faithful shepherd.
Look upon him, love him; he worships you.

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text: Good shepherd, tell this youth what ’tis to love.

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text:
It is to be all made of sighs and tears,
And so am I for Phoebe.

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text: And I for Ganymede.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: And I for Rosalind.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: And I for no woman.

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text:
It is to be all made of faith and service,
And so am I for Phoebe.

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text: And I for Ganymede.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: And I for Rosalind.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: And I for no woman.

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text:
It is to be all made of fantasy,
All made of passion and all made of wishes,
All adoration, duty, and observance,
All humbleness, all patience and impatience,
All purity, all trial, all observance,
And so am I for Phoebe.

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text: And so am I for Ganymede.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: And so am I for Rosalind.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: And so am I for no woman.

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text: If this be so, why blame you me to love you?

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text: If this be so, why blame you me to love you?

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: If this be so, why blame you me to love you?

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: Why do you speak, too, “Why blame you me to love you?”?

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: To her that is not here, nor doth not hear.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
Pray you, no more of this. 'Tis like the howling of
Irish wolves against the moon. [to SILVIUS] I will help
you, if I can. [to PHOEBE] I would love you if I
could.— Tomorrow meet me all together. [to PHOEBE] I
will marry you if ever I marry woman, and I’ll be
married tomorrow. [to ORLANDO] I will satisfy you if
ever I satisfy man, and you shall be married tomorrow.
[to SILVIUS] I will content you, if what pleases you
contents you, and you shall be married tomorrow. [to
ORLANDO] As you love Rosalind, meet. [to SILVIUS] As
you love Phoebe, meet.— And as I love no woman, I’ll
meet. So fare you well. I have left you commands.

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text: I’ll not fail, if I live.

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text: Nor I.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: Nor I.
Exeunt
Enter TOUCHSTONE and AUDREY.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
Tomorrow is the joyful day, Audrey. Tomorrow will we be
married.

units.speaker: AUDREY
units.text:
I do desire it with all my heart, and I hope it is no
dishonest desire to desire to be a woman of the world.
Enter two PAGES

units.speaker: AUDREY
units.text: Here comes two of the banished duke’s pages.

units.speaker: FIRST PAGE
units.text: Well met, honest gentleman.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text: By my troth, well met. Come, sit, sit, and a song.

units.speaker: SECOND PAGE
units.text: We are for you. Sit i' th' middle.

units.speaker: FIRST PAGE
units.text:
Shall we clap into ’t roundly, without hawking or
spitting or saying we are hoarse, which are the only
prologues to a bad voice?

units.speaker: SECOND PAGE
units.text:
I' faith, i' faith, and both in a tune like two gypsies
on a horse.

units.speaker: PAGES
units.text:
[sing]
It was a lover and his lass,
With a hey, and a ho, and a hey-nonny-no,
That o'er the green cornfield did pass
In springtime, the only pretty ring time,
When birds do sing, Hey ding a ding, ding.
Sweet lovers love the spring.
Between the acres of the rye,
With a hey, and a ho, and a hey-nonny-no,
These pretty country folks would lie
In springtime, the only pretty ring time,
When birds do sing, Hey ding a ding, ding.
Sweet lovers love the spring.
This carol they began that hour,
With a hey, and a ho, and a hey-nonny-no,
How that a life was but a flower
In springtime, the only pretty ring time,
When birds do sing, Hey ding a ding, ding.
Sweet lovers love the spring.
And therefore take the present time,
With a hey, and a ho, and a hhey-nonny-no,
For love is crownèd with the prime
In springtime, the only pretty ring time,
When birds do sing, Hey ding a ding, ding.
Sweet lovers love the spring.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
Truly, young gentlemen, though there was no great
matter in the ditty, yet the note was very untunable.

units.speaker: FIRST PAGE
units.text:
You are deceived, sir. We kept time. We lost not our
time.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
By my troth, yes. I count it but time lost to hear such
a foolish song. God be wi' you, and God mend your
voices.— Come, Audrey.
Exeunt
Enter DUKE SENIOR, AMIENS, JAQUES, ORLANDO, OLIVER, and CELIA

units.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
units.text:
Dost thou believe, Orlando, that the boy
Can do all this that he hath promisèd?

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text:
I sometimes do believe and sometimes do not,
As those that fear they hope, and know they fear.
Enter ROSALIND, SILVIUS, and PHOEBE

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
[as Ganymede] Patience once more whiles our compact is
urged.
[to DUKE SENIOR] You say, if I bring in your Rosalind,
You will bestow her on Orlando here?

units.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
units.text: That would I, had I kingdoms to give with her.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
[to ORLANDO] And you say you will have her when I bring
her?

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: That would I, were I of all kingdoms king.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text: [to PHOEBE] You say you’ll marry me if I be willing?

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text: That will I, should I die the hour after.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
But if you do refuse to marry me,
You’ll give yourself to this most faithful shepherd?

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text: So is the bargain.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
[to SILVIUS] You say that you’ll have Phoebe if she
will?

units.speaker: SILVIUS
units.text: Though to have her and death were both one thing.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
I have promised to make all this matter even.
Keep you your word, O duke, to give your daughter,
—You yours, Orlando, to receive his daughter.
—Keep your word, Phoebe, that you’ll marry me
Or else, refusing me, to wed this shepherd.
—Keep your word, Silvius, that you’ll marry her
If she refuse me. And from hence I go
To make these doubts all even.
Exeunt ROSALIND and CELIA

units.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
units.text:
I do remember in this shepherd boy
Some lively touches of my daughter’s favor.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text:
My lord, the first time that I ever saw him
Methought he was a brother to your daughter.
But, my good lord, this boy is forest-born
And hath been tutored in the rudiments
Of many desperate studies by his uncle,
Whom he reports to be a great magician
Obscurèd in the circle of this forest.
Enter TOUCHSTONE and AUDREY

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text:
There is sure another flood toward, and these couples
are coming to the ark. Here comes a pair of very strange
beasts, which in all tongues are called fools.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text: Salutation and greeting to you all.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text:
Good my lord, bid him welcome. This is the
motley-minded gentleman that I have so often met in the
forest. He hath been a courtier, he swears.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
If any man doubt that, let him put me to my purgation.
I have trod a measure. I have flattered a lady. I have
been politic with my friend, smooth with mine enemy. I
have undone three tailors. I have had four quarrels, and
like to have fought one.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text: And how was that ta'en up?

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
Faith, we met and found the quarrel was upon the
seventh cause.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text: How “seventh cause?”—Good my lord, like this fellow.

units.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
units.text: I like him very well.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
God 'ild you, sir. I desire you of the like. I press in
here, sir, amongst the rest of the country copulatives,
to swear and to forswear, according as marriage binds
and blood breaks. A poor virgin, sir, an ill-favored
thing, sir, but mine own. A poor humor of mine, sir, to
take that that no man else will. Rich honesty dwells
like a miser, sir, in a poor house, as your pearl in
your foul oyster.

units.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
units.text: By my faith, he is very swift and sententious.

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
According to the fool’s bolt, sir, and such dulcet
diseases.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text:
But for the seventh cause. How did you find the quarrel
on the seventh cause?

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
Upon a lie seven times removed.— Bear your body more
seeming, Audrey.— As thus, sir: I did dislike the cut of
a certain courtier’s beard. He sent me word if I said
his beard was not cut well, he was in the mind it was.
This is called “the retort courteous.” If I sent him word
again it was not well cut, he would send me word he cut
it to please himself. This is called “the quip modest.”
If again it was not well cut, he disabled my judgment.
This is called “the reply churlish.” If again it was not
well cut, he would answer I spake not true. This is
called “the reproof valiant.” If again it was not well
cut, he would say I lie. This is called “the
countercheck quarrelsome,” and so to “the lie
circumstantial” and “the lie direct.”

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text: And how oft did you say his beard was not well cut?

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
I durst go no further than the lie circumstantial, nor
he durst not give me the lie direct, and so we measured
swords and parted.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text: Can you nominate in order now the degrees of the lie?

units.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
units.text:
O sir, we quarrel in print, by the book, as you have
books for good manners. I will name you the degrees: the
first, “the retort courteous;” the second, “the quip
modest;” the third, “the reply churlish;” the fourth,
“the reproof valiant;” the fifth, “the countercheque
quarrelsome;” the sixth, “the lie with circumstance;”
the seventh, “the lie direct.” All these you may avoid
but the lie direct, and you may avoid that, too, with an
“if.” I knew when seven justices could not take up a
quarrel, but when the parties were met themselves, one
of them thought but of an “if,” as: “If you said so,
then I said so.” And they shook hands and swore
brothers. Your “if” is the only peacemaker: much virtue
in “if.”

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text:
Is not this a rare fellow, my lord? He’s as good at
anything and yet a fool.

units.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
units.text:
He uses his folly like a stalking-horse, and under the
presentation of that he shoots his wit.
Enter HYMEN, ROSALIND, and CELIA. Soft music

units.speaker: HYMEN
units.text:
Then is there mirth in heaven
When earthly things, made even,
Atone together.
Good duke, receive thy daughter.
Hymen from heaven brought her,
Yea, brought her hither,
That thou mightst join her hand with his
Whose heart within her bosom is.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
[to DUKE SENIOR] To you I give myself, for I am yours.
[to ORLANDO] To you I give myself, for I am yours.

units.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
units.text: If there be truth in sight, you are my daughter.

units.speaker: ORLANDO
units.text: If there be truth in sight, you are my Rosalind.

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text:
If sight and shape be true,
Why then, my love adieu.

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
[to DUKE SENIOR] I’ll have no father, if you be not he.
[to ORLANDO] I’ll have no husband, if you be not he,
[to PHOEBE] Nor ne'er wed woman, if you be not she.

units.speaker: HYMEN
units.text:
Peace, ho! I bar confusion.
'Tis I must make conclusion
Of these most strange events.
Here’s eight that must take hands
To join in Hymen’s bands,
If truth holds true contents.
[to ORLANDO and ROSALIND]
You and you no cross shall part.
[to OLIVER and CELIA]
You and you are heart in heart.
[to PHOEBE]
You to his love must accord
Or have a woman to your lord.
[to TOUCHSTONE and AUDREY]
You and you are sure together
As the winter to foul weather.
[to all]
Whiles a wedlock hymn we sing,
Feed yourselves with questioning,
That reason wonder may diminish
How thus we met, and these things finish.
[sings]
Wedding is great Juno’s crown,
O blessèd bond of board and bed.
'Tis Hymen peoples every town.
High wedlock then be honorèd.
Honor, high honor, and renown,
To Hymen, god of every town.

units.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
units.text:
O my dear niece, welcome thou art to me,
Even daughter, welcome in no less degree.

units.speaker: PHOEBE
units.text:
I will not eat my word. Now thou art mine,
Thy faith my fancy to thee doth combine.
Enter JAQUES DE BOYS

units.speaker: JAQUES DE BOYS
units.text:
Let me have audience for a word or two.
I am the second son of old Sir Rowland,
That bring these tidings to this fair assembly.
Duke Frederick, hearing how that every day
Men of great worth resorted to this forest,
Addressed a mighty power, which were on foot
In his own conduct, purposely to take
His brother here and put him to the sword.
And to the skirts of this wild wood he came,
Where, meeting with an old religious man,
After some question with him, was converted
Both from his enterprise and from the world,
His crown bequeathing to his banished brother,
And all their lands restored to them again
That were with him exiled. This to be true
I do engage my life.

units.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
units.text:
Welcome, young man.
Thou offer’st fairly to thy brothers' wedding:
To one his lands withheld, and to the other
A land itself at large, a potent dukedom.
—First, in this forest let us do those ends
That here were well begun and well begot,
And, after, every of this happy number
That have endured shrewd days and nights with us
Shall share the good of our returnèd fortune
According to the measure of their states.
Meantime, forget this new-fall'n dignity,
And fall into our rustic revelry.
—Play, music.— And you brides and bridegrooms all,
With measure heaped in joy to th' measures fall.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text:
Sir, by your patience: if I heard you rightly,
The duke hath put on a religious life
And thrown into neglect the pompous court.

units.speaker: JAQUES DE BOYS
units.text: He hath.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text:
To him will I. Out of these convertites
There is much matter to be heard and learned.
[to DUKE SENIOR]
You to your former honor I bequeath;
Your patience and your virtue well deserves it.
[to ORLANDO]
You to a love that your true faith doth merit.
[to OLIVER]
You to your land, and love, and great allies.
[to SILVIUS]
You to a long and well-deservèd bed.
[to TOUCHSTONE]
And you to wrangling, for thy loving voyage
Is but for two months victualled.— So to your pleasures.
I am for other than for dancing measures.

units.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
units.text: Stay, Jaques, stay.

units.speaker: JAQUES
units.text:
To see no pastime I. What you would have
I’ll stay to know at your abandoned cave.
Exit

units.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
units.text:
Proceed, proceed. We’ll so begin these rites
As we do trust they’ll end, in true delights.
Exeunt all but ROSALIND

units.speaker: ROSALIND
units.text:
It is not the fashion to see the lady the epilogue, but
it is no more unhandsome than to see the lord the
prologue. If it be true that good wine needs no bush,
’tis true that a good play needs no epilogue. Yet to
good wine they do use good bushes, and good plays prove
the better by the help of good epilogues. What a case am
I in, then, that am neither a good epilogue nor cannot
insinuate with you in the behalf of a good play. I am
not furnished like a beggar; therefore to beg will not
become me. My way is to conjure you, and I’ll begin with
the women. I charge you, O women, for the love you bear
to men, to like as much of this play as please you. And
I charge you, O men, for the love you bear to women—as
I perceive by your simpering, none of you hates
them—that between you and the women the play may please.
If I were a woman, I would kiss as many of you as had
beards that pleased me, complexions that liked me, and
breaths that I defied not. And I am sure as many as have
good beards, or good faces, or sweet breaths will, for
my kind offer, when I make curtsy, bid me farewell.
Exit

sentences.text: Enter ORLANDO and ADAM

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
As I remember, Adam, it was upon this fashion
bequeathed me by will but poor a thousand crowns, and,
as thou sayest, charged my brother on his blessing to
breed me well. And there begins my sadness. My brother
Jacques he keeps at school, and report speaks goldenly
of his profit. For my part, he keeps me rustically at
home or, to speak more properly, stays me here at home
unkept; for call you that “keeping” for a gentleman of
my birth that differs not from the stalling of an ox?
His horses are bred better, for, besides that they are
fair with their feeding, they are taught their manage
and, to that end, riders dearly hired. But I, his
brother, gain nothing under him but growth, for the
which his animals on his dunghills are as much bound to
him as I. Besides this nothing that he so plentifully
gives me, the something that nature gave me his
countenance seems to take from me. He lets me feed with
his hinds, bars me the place of a brother, and, as much
as in him lies, mines my gentility with my education.
This is it, Adam, that grieves me, and the spirit of my
father, which I think is within me, begins to mutiny
against this servitude. I will no longer endure it,
though yet I know no wise remedy how to avoid it.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:

bequeathed me by will but poor a thousand crowns, and,
as thou sayest, charged my brother on his blessing to
breed me well. And there begins my sadness. My brother

of his profit. For my part, he keeps me rustically at
home or, to speak more properly, stays me here at home

brother, gain nothing under him but growth, for the

This is it, Adam, that grieves me, and the spirit of my

though yet I know no wise remedy how to avoid it.

His horses are bred better, for, besides that they are

brother, gain nothing under him but growth, for the
which his animals on his dunghills are as much bound to
him as I. Besides this nothing that he so plentifully
gives me, the something that nature gave me his
countenance seems to take from me. He lets me feed with
his hinds, bars me the place of a brother, and, as much
as in him lies, mines my gentility with my education.
This is it, Adam, that grieves me, and the spirit of my
father, which I think is within me, begins to mutiny
against this servitude. I will no longer endure it,
though yet I know no wise remedy how to avoid it.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
As I remember, Adam, it was upon this fashion
bequeathed me by will but poor a thousand crowns, and,
as thou sayest, charged my brother on his blessing to
breed me well. And there begins my sadness. My brother
Jacques he keeps at school, and report speaks goldenly
of his profit. For my part, he keeps me rustically at
home or, to speak more properly, stays me here at home
unkept; for call you that “keeping” for a gentleman of
my birth that differs not from the stalling of an ox?
His horses are bred better, for, besides that they are
fair with their feeding, they are taught their manage
and, to that end, riders dearly hired. But I, his
brother, gain nothing under him but growth, for the
which his animals on his dunghills are as much bound to
him as I. Besides this nothing that he so plentifully
gives me, the something that nature gave me his

Enter OLIVER

sentences.speaker: ADAM
sentences.text: Yonder comes my master, your brother.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
Go apart, Adam, and thou shalt hear how he will shake
me up.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
Go apart, Adam, and thou shalt hear how he will shake
me up.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text: Now, sir, what make you here?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Nothing. I am not taught to make anything.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Nothing. I am not taught to make anything.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text: What mar you then, sir?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
Marry, sir, I am helping you to mar that which God
made, a poor unworthy brother of yours, with idleness.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text: Marry, sir, be better employed, and be naught awhile.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
Shall I keep your hogs and eat husks with them? What
prodigal portion have I spent that I should come to such
penury?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
Shall I keep your hogs and eat husks with them? What
prodigal portion have I spent that I should come to such
penury?

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text: Know you where you are, sir?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: O sir, very well: here in your orchard.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text: Know you before whom, sir?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
Ay, better than him I am before knows me. I know you
are my eldest brother, and in the gentle condition of
blood you should so know me. The courtesy of nations
allows you my better, in that you are the first-born,
but the same tradition takes not away my blood, were
there twenty brothers betwixt us. I have as much of my
father in me as you, albeit, I confess, your coming
before me is nearer to his reverence.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text: What, boy! [strikes him]

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
Come, come, elder brother, you are too young in this.
[seizes him]

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
Come, come, elder brother, you are too young in this.
[seizes him]

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text: Wilt thou lay hands on me, villain?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
I am no villain. I am the youngest son of Sir Rowland
de Boys. He was my father, and he is thrice a villain
that says such a father begot villains. Wert thou not my
brother, I would not take this hand from thy throat
till this other had pulled out thy tongue for saying so.
Thou hast railed on thyself.

sentences.speaker: ADAM
sentences.text:
Sweet masters, be patient. For your father’s
remembrance, be at accord.

sentences.speaker: ADAM
sentences.text:
Sweet masters, be patient. For your father’s
remembrance, be at accord.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text: Let me go, I say.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
I will not till I please. You shall hear me. My father
charged you in his will to give me good education. You
have trained me like a peasant, obscuring and hiding
from me all gentlemanlike qualities. The spirit of my
father grows strong in me, and I will no longer endure
it. Therefore allow me such exercises as may become a
gentleman, or give me the poor allottery my father left
me by testament. With that I will go buy my fortunes.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
And what wilt thou do—beg when that is spent? Well,
sir, get you in. I will not long be troubled with you.
You shall have some part of your will. I pray you leave
me.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
And what wilt thou do—beg when that is spent? Well,
sir, get you in. I will not long be troubled with you.
You shall have some part of your will. I pray you leave
me.

sentences.text:
And what wilt thou do—beg when that is spent? Well,

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
And what wilt thou do—beg when that is spent? Well,
sir, get you in. I will not long be troubled with you.
You shall have some part of your will. I pray you leave

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
I will no further offend you than becomes me for my
good.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text: Get you with him, you old dog.

sentences.speaker: ADAM
sentences.text:
Is “old dog” my reward? Most true, I have lost my
teeth in your service. God be with my old master. He
would not have spoke such a word.

sentences.speaker: ADAM
sentences.text:
Is “old dog” my reward? Most true, I have lost my
teeth in your service. God be with my old master. He
would not have spoke such a word.

sentences.speaker: ADAM
sentences.text:
Is “old dog” my reward? Most true, I have lost my
teeth in your service. God be with my old master. He
would not have spoke such a word.
Exeunt ORLANDO and ADAM

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Is it even so? Begin you to grow upon me? I will physic
your rankness and yet give no thousand crowns
neither.—Holla, Dennis!

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Is it even so? Begin you to grow upon me? I will physic
your rankness and yet give no thousand crowns
neither.—Holla, Dennis!

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:

your rankness and yet give no thousand crowns
neither.—Holla, Dennis!

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Is it even so? Begin you to grow upon me? I will physic
your rankness and yet give no thousand crowns
neither.—Holla, Dennis!
Enter DENNIS

sentences.speaker: DENNIS
sentences.text: Calls your Worship?

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Was not Charles, the duke’s wrestler, here to speak
with me?

sentences.speaker: DENNIS
sentences.text:
So please you, he is here at the door and importunes
access to you.

sentences.speaker: DENNIS
sentences.text:
So please you, he is here at the door and importunes
access to you.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text: Call him in.
Exit DENNIS

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text: 'Twill be a good way, and tomorrow the wrestling is.
Enter CHARLES

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text: Good morrow to your Worship.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Good Monsieur Charles, what’s the new news at the new
court?

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:
There’s no news at the court, sir, but the old news.
That is, the old duke is banished by his younger brother
the new duke, and three or four loving lords have put
themselves into voluntary exile with him, whose lands
and revenues enrich the new duke. Therefore he gives

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Can you tell if Rosalind, the duke’s daughter, be
banished with her father?

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:
Oh, no, for the duke’s daughter her cousin so loves
her, being ever from their cradles bred together, that
she would have followed her exile or have died to stay
behind her. She is at the court, and no less beloved of
her uncle than his own daughter, and never two ladies
loved as they do.

sentences.speaker: CHARLES

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text: Where will the old duke live?

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:
They say he is already in the Forest of Arden, and a
many merry men with him; and there they live like the
old Robin Hood of England. They say many young gentlemen
flock to him every day and fleet the time carelessly,
as they did in the golden world.

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text: What, you wrestle tomorrow before the new duke?

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:
Marry, do I, sir, and I came to acquaint you with a
matter. I am given, sir, secretly to understand that
your younger brother Orlando hath a disposition to come
in disguised against me to try a fall. Tomorrow, sir, I
wrestle for my credit, and he that escapes me without
some broken limb shall acquit him well. Your brother is
but young and tender, and, for your love I would be
loath to foil him, as I must for my own honor if he come
in. Therefore, out of my love to you, I came hither to
acquaint you withal, that either you might stay him from
his intendment or brook such disgrace well as he shall
run into, in that it is a thing of his own search and
altogether against my will.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Charles, I thank thee for thy love to me, which thou
shalt find I will most kindly requite. I had myself
notice of my brother’s purpose herein and have by
underhand means labored to dissuade him from it; but he
is resolute. I’ll tell thee, Charles: it is the
stubbornest young fellow of France, full of ambition, an
envious emulator of every man’s good parts, a secret
and villainous contriver against me his natural brother.
Therefore use thy discretion. I had as lief thou didst
break his neck as his finger. And thou wert best look to
’t, for if thou dost him any slight disgrace or if he
do not mightily grace himself on thee, he will practice
against thee by poison, entrap thee by some treacherous
device and never leave thee till he hath ta'en thy life
by some indirect means or other. For I assure thee—and
almost with tears I speak it—there is not one so young
and so villainous this day living. I speak but brotherly
of him, but should I anatomize him to thee as he is, I
must blush and weep, and thou must look pale and wonder.

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:
I am heartily glad I came hither to you. If he come
tomorrow, I’ll give him his payment. If ever he go alone
again, I’ll never wrestle for prize more. And so God
keep your Worship.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text: Farewell, good Charles.
Exit CHARLES

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Now will I stir this gamester. I hope I shall see an
end of him, for my soul—yet I know not why—hates nothing
more than he. Yet he’s gentle, never schooled and yet
learned, full of noble device, of all sorts enchantingly
beloved, and indeed so much in the heart of the world
and especially of my own people, who best know him, that
I am altogether misprized. But it shall not be so long;
this wrestler shall clear all. Nothing remains but that
I kindle the boy thither, which now I’ll go about.

Enter CELIA and ROSALIND

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: I pray thee, Rosalind, sweet my coz, be merry.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Dear Celia, I show more mirth than I am mistress of,
and would you yet I were merrier? Unless you could teach
me to forget a banished father, you must not learn me
how to remember any extraordinary pleasure.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Dear Celia, I show more mirth than I am mistress of,
and would you yet I were merrier? Unless you could teach
me to forget a banished father, you must not learn me
how to remember any extraordinary pleasure.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
Herein I see thou lov’st me not with the full weight
that I love thee. If my uncle, thy banished father, had
banished thy uncle, the duke my father, so thou hadst
been still with me, I could have taught my love to take
thy father for mine. So wouldst thou, if the truth of
thy love to me were so righteously tempered as mine is
to thee.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
Herein I see thou lov’st me not with the full weight

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
Herein I see thou lov’st me not with the full weight

thy love to me were so righteously tempered as mine is
to thee.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Well, I will forget the condition of my estate to
rejoice in yours.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
You know my father hath no child but I, nor none is
like to have, and, truly, when he dies, thou shalt be
his heir, for what he hath taken away from thy father
perforce, I will render thee again in affection. By mine
honor I will, and when I break that oath, let me turn
monster. Therefore, my sweet Rose, my dear Rose, be
merry.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
From henceforth I will, coz, and devise sports. Let me
see—what think you of falling in love?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
From henceforth I will, coz, and devise sports. Let me
see—what think you of falling in love?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
