sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
O most gentle Jupiter, what tedious homily of love have
you wearied your parishioners withal, and never cried,
“Have patience, good people.”

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
O most gentle Jupiter, what tedious homily of love have
you wearied your parishioners withal, and never cried,
“Have patience, good people.”

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
[as Aliena] How now?—Back, friends.—Shepherd, go off a
little.—Go with him, sirrah.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
[as Aliena] How now?—Back, friends.—Shepherd, go off a
little.—Go with him, sirrah.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Come, shepherd, let us make an honorable retreat,
though not with bag and baggage, yet with scrip and
scrippage.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Come, shepherd, let us make an honorable retreat,
though not with bag and baggage, yet with scrip and
scrippage.
Exeunt CORIN and TOUCHSTONE

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: Didst thou hear these verses?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Oh, yes, I heard them all, and more too, for some of
them had in them more feet than the verses would bear.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: That’s no matter. The feet might bear the verses.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: That’s no matter. The feet might bear the verses.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Ay, but the feet were lame and could not bear
themselves without the verse, and therefore stood lamely
in the verse.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
But didst thou hear without wondering how thy name
should be hanged and carved upon these trees?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I was seven of the nine days out of the wonder before
you came, for look here what I found on a palm tree. I
was never so berhymed since Pythagoras' time, that I was
an Irish rat, which I can hardly remember.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I was seven of the nine days out of the wonder before
you came, for look here what I found on a palm tree. I
was never so berhymed since Pythagoras' time, that I was
an Irish rat, which I can hardly remember.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: Trow you who hath done this?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Is it a man?

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
And a chain, that you once wore, about his neck. Change
you color?

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
And a chain, that you once wore, about his neck. Change
you color?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: I prithee, who?

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
O Lord, Lord, it is a hard matter for friends to meet,
but mountains may be removed with earthquakes and so
encounter.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
O Lord, Lord, it is a hard matter for friends to meet,
but mountains may be removed with earthquakes and so
encounter.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Nay, but who is it?

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: Is it possible?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Nay, I prithee now, with most petitionary vehemence,
tell me who it is.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
O wonderful, wonderful, and most wonderful wonderful,
and yet again wonderful, and after that, out of all
whooping!

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Good my complexion, dost thou think though I am
caparisoned like a man, I have a doublet and hose in my
disposition? One inch of delay more is a South Sea of
discovery. I prithee, tell me who is it quickly, and
speak apace. I would thou couldst stammer, that thou
might’st pour this concealed man out of thy mouth as
wine comes out of a narrow-mouthed bottle— either too
much at once, or none at all. I prithee take the cork
out of thy mouth, that I may drink thy tidings.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: So you may put a man in your belly.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Is he of God’s making? What manner of man? Is his head
worth a hat or his chin worth a beard?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Is he of God’s making? What manner of man? Is his head
worth a hat or his chin worth a beard?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Is he of God’s making? What manner of man? Is his head
worth a hat or his chin worth a beard?

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: Nay, he hath but a little beard.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Why, God will send more, if the man will be thankful.
Let me stay the growth of his beard, if thou delay me
not the knowledge of his chin.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Why, God will send more, if the man will be thankful.
Let me stay the growth of his beard, if thou delay me
not the knowledge of his chin.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
It is young Orlando, that tripped up the wrestler’s
heels and your heart both in an instant.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Nay, but the devil take mocking. Speak sad brow and
true maid.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Nay, but the devil take mocking. Speak sad brow and
true maid.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: I' faith, coz, ’tis he.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Orlando?

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: Orlando.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Alas the day, what shall I do with my doublet and
hose? What did he when thou saw’st him? What said he?
How looked he? Wherein went he? What makes him here? Did
he ask for me? Where remains he? How parted he with
thee? And when shalt thou see him again? Answer me in
one word.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
You must borrow me Gargantua’s mouth first. 'Tis a
word too great for any mouth of this age’s size. To say
ay and no to these particulars is more than to answer in
a catechism.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:

word too great for any mouth of this age’s size. To say

a catechism.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
But doth he know that I am in this forest and in man’s
apparel? Looks he as freshly as he did the day he
wrestled?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
But doth he know that I am in this forest and in man’s
apparel? Looks he as freshly as he did the day he
wrestled?

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
It is as easy to count atomies as to resolve the
propositions of a lover. But take a taste of my finding
him, and relish it with good observance. I found him
under a tree like a dropped acorn.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
It is as easy to count atomies as to resolve the
propositions of a lover. But take a taste of my finding
him, and relish it with good observance. I found him

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
It is as easy to count atomies as to resolve the
propositions of a lover. But take a taste of my finding
him, and relish it with good observance. I found him
under a tree like a dropped acorn.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
It may well be called Jove’s tree when it drops forth
such fruit.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: Give me audience, good madam.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Proceed.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: There lay he, stretched along like a wounded knight.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Though it be pity to see such a sight, it well becomes
the ground.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
Cry “holla” to thy tongue, I prithee. It curvets
unseasonably. He was furnished like a hunter.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
Cry “holla” to thy tongue, I prithee. It curvets
unseasonably. He was furnished like a hunter.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
Cry “holla” to thy tongue, I prithee. It curvets
unseasonably. He was furnished like a hunter.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Oh, ominous! He comes to kill my heart.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Oh, ominous! He comes to kill my heart.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
I would sing my song without a burden. Thou bring’st
me out of tune.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
I would sing my song without a burden. Thou bring’st
me out of tune.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Do you not know I am a woman? When I think, I must
speak. Sweet, say on.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Do you not know I am a woman? When I think, I must
speak. Sweet, say on.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Do you not know I am a woman? When I think, I must
speak. Sweet, say on.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: You bring me out. Soft, comes he not here?

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: You bring me out. Soft, comes he not here?
Enter ORLANDO and JAQUES

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: 'Tis he. Slink by, and note him.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: 'Tis he. Slink by, and note him.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
I thank you for your company, but, good faith, I had as
lief have been myself alone.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
I thank you for your company, but, good faith, I had as
lief have been myself alone.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
And so had I, but yet, for fashion sake, I thank you
too for your society.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
And so had I, but yet, for fashion sake, I thank you
too for your society.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: God be wi' you. Let’s meet as little as we can.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: God be wi' you. Let’s meet as little as we can.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: I do desire we may be better strangers.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
I pray you mar no more trees with writing love songs in
their barks.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
I pray you mar no more of my verses with reading them
ill- favoredly.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: Rosalind is your love’s name?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Yes, just.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: I do not like her name.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
There was no thought of pleasing you when she was
christened.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: What stature is she of?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Just as high as my heart.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
You are full of pretty answers. Have you not been
acquainted with goldsmiths' wives and conned them out of
rings?

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
You are full of pretty answers. Have you not been
acquainted with goldsmiths' wives and conned them out of
rings?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
Not so. But I answer you right painted cloth, from
whence you have studied your questions.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
You have a nimble wit. I think ’twas made of Atalanta’s
heels. Will you sit down with me? And we two will rail
against our mistress the world and all our misery.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
You have a nimble wit. I think ’twas made of Atalanta’s
heels. Will you sit down with me? And we two will rail
against our mistress the world and all our misery.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
You have a nimble wit. I think ’twas made of Atalanta’s

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
You have a nimble wit. I think ’twas made of Atalanta’s
heels. Will you sit down with me? And we two will rail
against our mistress the world and all our misery.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
I will chide no breather in the world but myself,
against whom I know most faults.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: The worst fault you have is to be in love.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
'Tis a fault I will not change for your best virtue. I
am weary of you.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
'Tis a fault I will not change for your best virtue. I
am weary of you.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: By my troth, I was seeking for a fool when I found you.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
He is drowned in the brook. Look but in, and you shall
see him.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
He is drowned in the brook. Look but in, and you shall
see him.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: There I shall see mine own figure.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Which I take to be either a fool or a cipher.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
I’ll tarry no longer with you. Farewell, good Signior
Love.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
I’ll tarry no longer with you. Farewell, good Signior
Love.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
I am glad of your departure. Adieu, good Monsieur
Melancholy.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
I am glad of your departure. Adieu, good Monsieur
Melancholy.
Exit JAQUES

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
[aside to CELIA] I will speak to him like a saucy
lackey, and under that habit play the knave with him.—Do
you hear, forester?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Very well. What would you?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Very well. What would you?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: [As Ganymede) I pray you, what is ’t o'clock?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
You should ask me what time o' day. There’s no clock
in the forest.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
You should ask me what time o' day. There’s no clock
in the forest.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Then there is no true lover in the forest, else sighing
every minute and groaning every hour would detect the
lazy foot of time as well as a clock.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
And why not the swift foot of time? Had not that been
as proper?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
And why not the swift foot of time? Had not that been
as proper?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
By no means, sir. Time travels in diverse paces with
diverse persons. I’ll tell you who time ambles withal,
who time trots withal, who time gallops withal, and who
he stands still withal.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
By no means, sir. Time travels in diverse paces with
diverse persons. I’ll tell you who time ambles withal,
who time trots withal, who time gallops withal, and who
he stands still withal.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
By no means, sir. Time travels in diverse paces with
diverse persons. I’ll tell you who time ambles withal,
who time trots withal, who time gallops withal, and who
he stands still withal.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: I prithee, who doth he trot withal?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Marry, he trots hard with a young maid between the
contract of her marriage and the day it is solemnized.
If the interim be but a se'nnight, time’s pace is so hard
that it seems the length of seven year.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Marry, he trots hard with a young maid between the
contract of her marriage and the day it is solemnized.
If the interim be but a se'nnight, time’s pace is so hard
that it seems the length of seven year.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Who ambles time withal?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
With a priest that lacks Latin and a rich man that hath
not the gout, for the one sleeps easily because he
cannot study and the other lives merrily because he
feels no pain— the one lacking the burden of lean and
wasteful learning, the other knowing no burden of heavy
tedious penury. These time ambles withal.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
With a priest that lacks Latin and a rich man that hath
not the gout, for the one sleeps easily because he
cannot study and the other lives merrily because he

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Who doth he gallop withal?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
With a thief to the gallows, for though he go as softly
as foot can fall, he thinks himself too soon there.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
With a thief to the gallows, for though he go as softly
as foot can fall, he thinks himself too soon there.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Who stays it still withal?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
With lawyers in the vacation, for they sleep between
term and term, and then they perceive not how time
moves.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Where dwell you, pretty youth?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
With this shepherdess, my sister, here in the skirts
of the forest like fringe upon a petticoat.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Are you native of this place?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: As the cony that you see dwell where she is kindled.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
Your accent is something finer than you could purchase
in so removed a dwelling.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I have been told so of many. But indeed an old
religious uncle of mine taught me to speak, who was in
his youth an inland man, one that knew courtship too
well, for there he fell in love. I have heard him read
many lectures against it, and I thank God I am not a
woman, to be touched with so many giddy offenses as he
hath generally taxed their whole sex withal.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:

religious uncle of mine taught me to speak, who was in

well, for there he fell in love. I have heard him read

religious uncle of mine taught me to speak, who was in

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
Can you remember any of the principal evils that he
laid to the charge of women?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
There were none principal. They were all like one
another as half-pence are, every one fault seeming
monstrous till his fellow fault came to match it.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
There were none principal. They were all like one
another as half-pence are, every one fault seeming
monstrous till his fellow fault came to match it.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: I prithee, recount some of them.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
No, I will not cast away my physic but on those that
are sick. There is a man haunts the forest that abuses
our young plants with carving “Rosalind” on their barks,
hangs odes upon hawthorns and elegies on brambles, all,
forsooth, deifying the name of Rosalind. If I could
meet that fancy-monger I would give him some good
counsel, for he seems to have the quotidian of love upon
him.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
No, I will not cast away my physic but on those that
are sick. There is a man haunts the forest that abuses
our young plants with carving “Rosalind” on their barks,
hangs odes upon hawthorns and elegies on brambles, all,
forsooth, deifying the name of Rosalind. If I could
meet that fancy-monger I would give him some good
counsel, for he seems to have the quotidian of love upon

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
No, I will not cast away my physic but on those that
are sick. There is a man haunts the forest that abuses

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
I am he that is so love-shaked. I pray you tell me your
remedy.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
I am he that is so love-shaked. I pray you tell me your
remedy.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
There is none of my uncle’s marks upon you. He taught
me how to know a man in love, in which cage of rushes I
am sure you are not prisoner.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
There is none of my uncle’s marks upon you. He taught
me how to know a man in love, in which cage of rushes I
am sure you are not prisoner.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: What were his marks?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
A lean cheek, which you have not; a blue eye and
sunken, which you have not; an unquestionable spirit,
which you have not; a beard neglected, which you have
not— but I pardon you for that, for simply your having in
beard is a younger brother’s revenue. Then your hose
should be ungartered, your bonnet unbanded, your sleeve
unbuttoned, your shoe untied, and everything about you
demonstrating a careless desolation. But you are no such
man. You are rather point-device in your accouterments,
as loving yourself than seeming the lover of any other.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Fair youth, I would I could make thee believe I love.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Me believe it? You may as soon make her that you love
believe it, which I warrant she is apter to do than to
confess she does. That is one of the points in the which
women still give the lie to their consciences. But, in
good sooth, are you he that hangs the verses on the
trees wherein Rosalind is so admired?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
I swear to thee, youth, by the white hand of Rosalind,
I am that he, that unfortunate he.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: But are you so much in love as your rhymes speak?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Neither rhyme nor reason can express how much.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Love is merely a madness and, I tell you, deserves as
well a dark house and a whip as madmen do, and the
reason why they are not so punished and cured is that
the lunacy is so ordinary that the whippers are in love,
too. Yet I profess curing it by counsel.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Love is merely a madness and, I tell you, deserves as
well a dark house and a whip as madmen do, and the
reason why they are not so punished and cured is that
the lunacy is so ordinary that the whippers are in love,
too. Yet I profess curing it by counsel.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Love is merely a madness and, I tell you, deserves as

the lunacy is so ordinary that the whippers are in love,

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Did you ever cure any so?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Yes, one, and in this manner. He was to imagine me his
love, his mistress, and I set him every day to woo me;
at which time would I, being but a moonish youth,
grieve, be effeminate, changeable, longing and liking,
proud, fantastical, apish, shallow, inconstant, full of
tears, full of smiles; for every passion something, and
for no passion truly anything, as boys and women are,
for the most part, cattle of this color; would now like
him, now loathe him; then entertain him, then forswear
him; now weep for him, then spit at him, that I drave my
suitor from his mad humor of love to a living humor of
madness, which was to forswear the full stream of the
world and to live in a nook merely monastic. And thus I
cured him, and this way will I take upon me to wash your
liver as clean as a sound sheep’s heart, that there
shall not be one spot of love in ’t.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: I would not be cured, youth.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I would cure you if you would but call me Rosalind and
come every day to my cote and woo me.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
Now, by the faith of my love, I will. Tell me where it
is.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
Now, by the faith of my love, I will. Tell me where it
is.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Go with me to it, and I’ll show it you; and by the way
you shall tell me where in the forest you live. Will
you go?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Go with me to it, and I’ll show it you; and by the way
you shall tell me where in the forest you live. Will
you go?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Go with me to it, and I’ll show it you; and by the way
you shall tell me where in the forest you live. Will
you go?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: With all my heart, good youth.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Nay, you must call me Rosalind.—Come, sister, will you
go?
Exeunt
Enter TOUCHSTONE and AUDREY, and JAQUES behind

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Come apace, good Audrey. I will fetch up your goats,
Audrey. And how, Audrey? Am I the man yet? Doth my
simple feature content you?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Come apace, good Audrey. I will fetch up your goats,
Audrey. And how, Audrey? Am I the man yet? Doth my
simple feature content you?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Come apace, good Audrey. I will fetch up your goats,
Audrey. And how, Audrey? Am I the man yet? Doth my
simple feature content you?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Come apace, good Audrey. I will fetch up your goats,
Audrey. And how, Audrey? Am I the man yet? Doth my
simple feature content you?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Come apace, good Audrey. I will fetch up your goats,
Audrey. And how, Audrey? Am I the man yet? Doth my
simple feature content you?

sentences.speaker: AUDREY
sentences.text: Your features, Lord warrant us! What features?

sentences.speaker: AUDREY
sentences.text: Your features, Lord warrant us! What features?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
I am here with thee and thy goats, as the most
capricious poet, honest Ovid, was among the Goths.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
[aside] O knowledge ill-inhabited, worse than Jove in a
thatched house.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
When a man’s verses cannot be understood nor a man’s
good wit seconded with the forward child, understanding,
it strikes a man more dead than a great reckoning in a
little room. Truly, I would the gods had made thee
poetical.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
When a man’s verses cannot be understood nor a man’s
good wit seconded with the forward child, understanding,
it strikes a man more dead than a great reckoning in a
little room. Truly, I would the gods had made thee
poetical.

sentences.speaker: AUDREY
sentences.text:
I do not know what “poetical” is. Is it honest in deed
and word? Is it a true thing?

sentences.speaker: AUDREY
sentences.text:
I do not know what “poetical” is. Is it honest in deed
and word? Is it a true thing?

sentences.speaker: AUDREY
sentences.text:
I do not know what “poetical” is. Is it honest in deed
and word? Is it a true thing?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
No, truly, for the truest poetry is the most feigning,
and lovers are given to poetry, and what they swear in
poetry may be said as lovers they do feign.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
No, truly, for the truest poetry is the most feigning,
and lovers are given to poetry, and what they swear in
poetry may be said as lovers they do feign.

sentences.speaker: AUDREY
sentences.text: Do you wish then that the gods had made me poetical?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
I do, truly, for thou swear’st to me thou art honest.
Now, if thou wert a poet, I might have some hope thou
didst feign.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
I do, truly, for thou swear’st to me thou art honest.
Now, if thou wert a poet, I might have some hope thou
didst feign.

sentences.speaker: AUDREY
sentences.text: Would you not have me honest?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
No, truly, unless thou wert hard-favored, for honesty
coupled to beauty is to have honey a sauce to sugar.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
No, truly, unless thou wert hard-favored, for honesty
coupled to beauty is to have honey a sauce to sugar.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: [aside] A material fool.

sentences.speaker: AUDREY
sentences.text:
Well, I am not fair, and therefore I pray the gods make
me honest.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Truly, and to cast away honesty upon a foul slut were
to put good meat into an unclean dish.

sentences.speaker: AUDREY
sentences.text: I am not a slut, though I thank the gods I am foul.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Well, praised be the gods for thy foulness;
sluttishness may come hereafter. But be it as it may be,
I will marry thee; and to that end I have been with Sir
Oliver Martext, the vicar of the next village, who hath
promised to meet me in this place of the forest and to
couple us.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:

promised to meet me in this place of the forest and to

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:

sluttishness may come hereafter. But be it as it may be,

promised to meet me in this place of the forest and to
couple us.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: [aside] I would fain see this meeting.

sentences.speaker: AUDREY
sentences.text: Well, the gods give us joy.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Amen. A man may, if he were of a fearful heart, stagger
in this attempt, for here we have no temple but the
wood, no assembly but horn-beasts. But what though?
Courage. As horns are odious, they are necessary. It is
said, “Many a man knows no end of his goods.” Right:
many a man has good horns and knows no end of
them. Well, that is the dowry of his wife; ’tis none of
his own getting. Horns? Even so. Poor men alone? No, no.
The noblest deer hath them as huge as the rascal. Is
the single man therefore blessed? No. As a walled town
is more worthier than a village, so is the forehead of a
married man more honorable than the bare brow of a
bachelor. And by how much defense is better than no
skill, by so much is a horn more precious than to want.

is more worthier than a village, so is the forehead of a
married man more honorable than the bare brow of a
bachelor. And by how much defense is better than no
skill, by so much is a horn more precious than to want.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Amen. A man may, if he were of a fearful heart, stagger
in this attempt, for here we have no temple but the
wood, no assembly but horn-beasts. But what though?
Courage. As horns are odious, they are necessary. It is
said, “Many a man knows no end of his goods.” Right:
many a man has good horns and knows no end of
them. Well, that is the dowry of his wife; ’tis none of
his own getting. Horns? Even so. Poor men alone? No, no.
The noblest deer hath them as huge as the rascal. Is
the single man therefore blessed? No. As a walled town
is more worthier than a village, so is the forehead of a
married man more honorable than the bare brow of a
bachelor. And by how much defense is better than no
skill, by so much is a horn more precious than to want.

sentences.speaker: SIR OLIVER MARTEXT
sentences.text: Is there none here to give the woman?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text: I will not take her on gift of any man.

sentences.speaker: SIR OLIVER MARTEXT
sentences.text:
Truly, she must be given, or the marriage is not
lawful.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: [advancing] Proceed, proceed. I’ll give her.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: [advancing] Proceed, proceed. I’ll give her.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Good even, good Monsieur What-ye-call’t. How do you,
sir? You are very well met. God 'ild you for your last
company. I am very glad to see you. Even a toy in hand
here, sir. Nay, pray be covered.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: Will you be married, motley?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
As the ox hath his bow, sir, the horse his curb, and
the falcon her bells, so man hath his desires; and as
pigeons bill, so wedlock would be nibbling.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
And will you, being a man of your breeding, be married
under a bush like a beggar? Get you to church, and have
a good priest that can tell you what marriage is. This
fellow will but join you together as they join wainscot.
Then one of you will prove a shrunk panel and, like
green timber, warp, warp.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
[aside] I am not in the mind but I were better to be
married of him than of another, for he is not like to
marry me well, and not being well married, it will be a
good excuse for me hereafter to leave my wife.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
[aside] I am not in the mind but I were better to be

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: Go thou with me, and let me counsel thee.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Come, sweet Audrey.
We must be married, or we must live in bawdry.—
Farewell, good Master Oliver, not
O sweet Oliver,
O brave Oliver,
Leave me not behind thee
But
Wind away,
Begone, I say,
I will not to wedding with thee.

sentences.speaker: SIR OLIVER MARTEXT
sentences.text:
'Tis no matter. Ne'er a fantastical knave of them all
shall flout me out of my calling.

sentences.speaker: SIR OLIVER MARTEXT
sentences.text:
'Tis no matter. Ne'er a fantastical knave of them all
shall flout me out of my calling.
Exit
Enter ROSALIND and CELIA

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Never talk to me. I will weep.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Never talk to me. I will weep.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
Do, I prithee, but yet have the grace to consider that
tears do not become a man.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: But have I not cause to weep?

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: As good cause as one would desire. Therefore weep.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: As good cause as one would desire. Therefore weep.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: His very hair is of the dissembling color.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
Something browner than Judas’s. Marry, his kisses are
Judas’s own children.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
Something browner than Judas’s. Marry, his kisses are
Judas’s own children.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: I' faith, his hair is of a good color.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
An excellent color. Your chestnut was ever the only
color.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
An excellent color. Your chestnut was ever the only
color.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
And his kissing is as full of sanctity as the touch of
holy bread.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
He hath bought a pair of cast lips of Diana. A nun of
winter’s sisterhood kisses not more religiously. The
very ice of chastity is in them.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
He hath bought a pair of cast lips of Diana. A nun of
winter’s sisterhood kisses not more religiously. The
very ice of chastity is in them.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
He hath bought a pair of cast lips of Diana. A nun of
winter’s sisterhood kisses not more religiously. The
very ice of chastity is in them.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
But why did he swear he would come this morning, and
comes not?

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: Nay, certainly, there is no truth in him.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Do you think so?

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
Yes, I think he is not a pick-purse nor a
horse-stealer, but for his verity in love, I do think
him as concave as a covered goblet or a worm-eaten nut.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
Yes, I think he is not a pick-purse nor a
horse-stealer, but for his verity in love, I do think
him as concave as a covered goblet or a worm-eaten nut.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Not true in love?

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: Yes, when he is in, but I think he is not in.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: You have heard him swear downright he was.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
“Was” is not “is.” Besides, the oath of a lover is no
stronger than the word of a tapster. They are both the
confirmer of false reckonings. He attends here in the
forest on the duke your father.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
“Was” is not “is.” Besides, the oath of a lover is no
stronger than the word of a tapster. They are both the
confirmer of false reckonings. He attends here in the
forest on the duke your father.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
“Was” is not “is.” Besides, the oath of a lover is no

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I met the duke yesterday and had much question with
him. He asked me of what parentage I was. I told him, of
as good as he. So he laughed and let me go. But what
talk we of fathers when there is such a man as Orlando?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:

him. He asked me of what parentage I was. I told him, of
as good as he. So he laughed and let me go. But what
talk we of fathers when there is such a man as Orlando?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I met the duke yesterday and had much question with
him. He asked me of what parentage I was. I told him, of
as good as he. So he laughed and let me go. But what
talk we of fathers when there is such a man as Orlando?

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
Oh, that’s a brave man. He writes brave verses, speaks
brave words, swears brave oaths, and breaks them
bravely, quite traverse, athwart the heart of his lover,
as a puny tilter that spurs his horse but on one side
breaks his staff like a noble goose; but all’s brave
that youth mounts and folly guides.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:

Enter CORIN

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: Who comes here?

sentences.speaker: CORIN
sentences.text:
Mistress and master, you have oft inquired
After the shepherd that complained of love,
Who you saw sitting by me on the turf,
Praising the proud disdainful shepherdess
That was his mistress.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: [As Aliena] Well, and what of him?

sentences.speaker: CORIN
sentences.text:
If you will see a pageant truly played
Between the pale complexion of true love
And the red glow of scorn and proud disdain,
Go hence a little, and I shall conduct you,
If you will mark it.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
[aside to CELIA] O, come, let us remove.
The sight of lovers feedeth those in love.
[as Ganymede, to CORIN] Bring us to this sight, and you
shall say
I’ll prove a busy actor in their play.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
[aside to CELIA] O, come, let us remove.
The sight of lovers feedeth those in love.
[as Ganymede, to CORIN] Bring us to this sight, and you
shall say
I’ll prove a busy actor in their play.
Exeunt
Enter SILVIUS and PHOEBE

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text:
Sweet Phoebe, do not scorn me. Do not, Phoebe.
Say that you love me not, but say not so
In bitterness. The common executioner,
Whose heart th' accustomed sight of death makes hard,
Falls not the axe upon the humbled neck
But first begs pardon. Will you sterner be
Than he that dies and lives by bloody drops?

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text:

Enter ROSALIND, CELIA, and CORIN, behind

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text:
I would not be thy executioner.
I fly thee, for I would not injure thee.
Thou tell’st me there is murder in mine eye.
'Tis pretty, sure, and very probable
That eyes, that are the frail’st and softest things,
Who shut their coward gates on atomies,
Should be called tyrants, butchers, murderers.
Now I do frown on thee with all my heart,
And if mine eyes can wound, now let them kill thee.
Now counterfeit to swoon, why, now fall down;
Or if thou canst not, Oh, for shame, for shame,
Lie not, to say mine eyes are murderers.
Now show the wound mine eye hath made in thee.
Scratch thee but with a pin, and there remains
Some scar of it. Lean upon a rush,
The cicatrice and capable impressure
Thy palm some moment keeps. But now mine eyes,
Which I have darted at thee, hurt thee not.
Nor, I am sure, there is no force in eyes
That can do hurt.

Which I have darted at thee, hurt thee not.
Nor, I am sure, there is no force in eyes
That can do hurt.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text:
I would not be thy executioner.
I fly thee, for I would not injure thee.
Thou tell’st me there is murder in mine eye.
'Tis pretty, sure, and very probable
That eyes, that are the frail’st and softest things,
Who shut their coward gates on atomies,
Should be called tyrants, butchers, murderers.
Now I do frown on thee with all my heart,
And if mine eyes can wound, now let them kill thee.
Now counterfeit to swoon, why, now fall down;
Or if thou canst not, Oh, for shame, for shame,
Lie not, to say mine eyes are murderers.
Now show the wound mine eye hath made in thee.
Scratch thee but with a pin, and there remains
Some scar of it. Lean upon a rush,
The cicatrice and capable impressure
Thy palm some moment keeps. But now mine eyes,
Which I have darted at thee, hurt thee not.
Nor, I am sure, there is no force in eyes
That can do hurt.

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text:
O dear Phoebe,
If ever—as that ever may be near—
You meet in some fresh cheek the power of fancy,
Then shall you know the wounds invisible
That love’s keen arrows make.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text:
But till that time
Come not thou near me. And when that time comes,
Afflict me with thy mocks, pity me not,
As till that time I shall not pity thee.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
[Advancing, as Ganymede] And why, I pray you? Who might
be your mother,
That you insult, exult, and all at once,
Over the wretched? What though you have no beauty—
As, by my faith, I see no more in you
Than without candle may go dark to bed—
Must you be therefore proud and pitiless?
Why, what means this? Why do you look on me?
I see no more in you than in the ordinary
Of nature’s sale-work.— 'Od’s my little life,
I think she means to tangle my eyes, too.
—No, faith, proud mistress, hope not after it.
'Tis not your inky brows, your black silk hair,
Your bugle eyeballs, nor your cheek of cream
That can entame my spirits to your worship.
—You foolish shepherd, wherefore do you follow her,
Like foggy south puffing with wind and rain?
You are a thousand times a properer man
Than she a woman. 'Tis such fools as you
That makes the world full of ill-favored children.
'Tis not her glass but you that flatters her,
And out of you she sees herself more proper
Than any of her lineaments can show her.
—But, mistress, know yourself. Down on your knees
And thank heaven, fasting, for a good man’s love,
For I must tell you friendly in your ear,
Sell when you can; you are not for all markets.
Cry the man mercy, love him, take his offer.
Foul is most foul, being foul to be a scoffer.
—So take her to thee, shepherd. Fare you well.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE

Sweet youth, I pray you chide a year together.
I had rather hear you chide than this man woo.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
He’s fall'n in love with your foulness. [to SILVIUS]
And she’ll fall in love with my anger. If it be so, as
fast as she answers thee with frowning looks, I’ll sauce
her with bitter words. [to PHOEBE] Why look you so upon
me?

fast as she answers thee with frowning looks, I’ll sauce

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text: For no ill will I bear you.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I pray you, do not fall in love with me,
For I am falser than vows made in wine.
Besides, I like you not. If you will know my house,
'Tis at the tuft of olives, here hard by.
—Will you go, sister?— Shepherd, ply her hard.
—Come, sister.— Shepherdess, look on him better,
And be not proud. Though all the world could see,
None could be so abused in sight as he.
—Come, to our flock.

Besides, I like you not. If you will know my house,
'Tis at the tuft of olives, here hard by.
—Will you go, sister?— Shepherd, ply her hard.
—Come, sister.— Shepherdess, look on him better,
And be not proud. Though all the world could see,
None could be so abused in sight as he.
—Come, to our flock.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text:
Dead shepherd, now I find thy saw of might:
“Who ever loved that loved not at first sight?”

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text: Sweet Phoebe—

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text: Ha, what sayst thou, Silvius?

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text: Sweet Phoebe, pity me.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text: Why, I am sorry for thee, gentle Silvius.

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text:
Wherever sorrow is, relief would be.
If you do sorrow at my grief in love,
By giving love your sorrow and my grief
Were both extermined.

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text:
Wherever sorrow is, relief would be.
If you do sorrow at my grief in love,
By giving love your sorrow and my grief
Were both extermined.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text: Thou hast my love. Is not that neighborly?

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text: Thou hast my love. Is not that neighborly?

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text: I would have you.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text:
Why, that were covetousness.
Silvius, the time was that I hated thee,
And yet it is not that I bear thee love,
But since that thou canst talk of love so well,
Thy company, which erst was irksome to me,
I will endure, and I’ll employ thee too.
But do not look for further recompense
Than thine own gladness that thou art employed.

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text:
So holy and so perfect is my love,
And I in such a poverty of grace,
That I shall think it a most plenteous crop
To glean the broken ears after the man
That the main harvest reaps. Loose now and then
A scattered smile, and that I’ll live upon.

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text:
So holy and so perfect is my love,

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text: Know’st thou the youth that spoke to me erewhile?

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text:
Not very well, but I have met him oft,
And he hath bought the cottage and the bounds
That the old carlot once was master of.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text:
Think not I love him, though I ask for him.
'Tis but a peevish boy—yet he talks well—
But what care I for words? Yet words do well
When he that speaks them pleases those that hear.
It is a pretty youth—not very pretty—
But sure he’s proud—and yet his pride becomes him.
He’ll make a proper man. The best thing in him
Is his complexion; and faster than his tongue
Did make offense, his eye did heal it up.
He is not very tall—yet for his years he’s tall.
His leg is but so-so—and yet ’tis well.
There was a pretty redness in his lip,
A little riper and more lusty red
Than that mixed in his cheek: ’twas just the difference
Betwixt the constant red and mingled damask.
There be some women, Silvius, had they marked him
In parcels as I did, would have gone near
To fall in love with him; but for my part
I love him not nor hate him not; and yet
I have more cause to hate him than to love him.
For what had he to do to chide at me?
He said mine eyes were black and my hair black
And, now I am remembered, scorned at me.
I marvel why I answered not again.
But that’s all one: omittance is no quittance.
I’ll write to him a very taunting letter,
And thou shalt bear it. Wilt thou, Silvius?

His leg is but so-so—and yet ’tis well.
There was a pretty redness in his lip,
A little riper and more lusty red
Than that mixed in his cheek: ’twas just the difference
Betwixt the constant red and mingled damask.
There be some women, Silvius, had they marked him
In parcels as I did, would have gone near
To fall in love with him; but for my part
I love him not nor hate him not; and yet
I have more cause to hate him than to love him.
For what had he to do to chide at me?
He said mine eyes were black and my hair black
And, now I am remembered, scorned at me.
I marvel why I answered not again.
But that’s all one: omittance is no quittance.
I’ll write to him a very taunting letter,
And thou shalt bear it. Wilt thou, Silvius?

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text:
Think not I love him, though I ask for him.
'Tis but a peevish boy—yet he talks well—
But what care I for words? Yet words do well
When he that speaks them pleases those that hear.
It is a pretty youth—not very pretty—
But sure he’s proud—and yet his pride becomes him.
He’ll make a proper man. The best thing in him
Is his complexion; and faster than his tongue
Did make offense, his eye did heal it up.
He is not very tall—yet for his years he’s tall.
His leg is but so-so—and yet ’tis well.
There was a pretty redness in his lip,
A little riper and more lusty red
Than that mixed in his cheek: ’twas just the difference
Betwixt the constant red and mingled damask.
There be some women, Silvius, had they marked him
In parcels as I did, would have gone near
To fall in love with him; but for my part
I love him not nor hate him not; and yet
I have more cause to hate him than to love him.
For what had he to do to chide at me?
He said mine eyes were black and my hair black
And, now I am remembered, scorned at me.
I marvel why I answered not again.
But that’s all one: omittance is no quittance.
I’ll write to him a very taunting letter,
And thou shalt bear it. Wilt thou, Silvius?

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text: Phoebe, with all my heart.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text:
I’ll write it straight.
The matter’s in my head and in my heart.
I will be bitter with him and passing short.
Go with me, Silvius.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text:
I’ll write it straight.
The matter’s in my head and in my heart.
I will be bitter with him and passing short.
Go with me, Silvius.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text:
I’ll write it straight.
The matter’s in my head and in my heart.
I will be bitter with him and passing short.
Go with me, Silvius.
Exeunt
Enter ROSALIND, CELIA, and JAQUES

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
I prithee, pretty youth, let me be better acquainted
with thee.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: They say you are a melancholy fellow.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: I am so. I do love it better than laughing.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: I am so. I do love it better than laughing.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Those that are in extremity of either are abominable
fellows and betray themselves to every modern censure
worse than drunkards.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: Why, ’tis good to be sad and say nothing.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Why then, ’tis good to be a post.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
I have neither the scholar’s melancholy, which is
emulation; nor the musician’s, which is fantastical; nor
the courtier’s, which is proud; nor the soldier’s,
which is ambitious; nor the lawyer’s, which is politic;
nor the lady’s, which is nice; nor the lover’s, which is
all these, but it is a melancholy of mine own,
compounded of many simples, extracted from many objects,
and indeed the sundry contemplation of my travels, in
which my often rumination wraps me in a most humorous
sadness.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
A traveler. By my faith, you have great reason to be
sad. I fear you have sold your own lands to see other
men’s. Then to have seen much and to have nothing is to
have rich eyes and poor hands.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:

have rich eyes and poor hands.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: Yes, I have gained my experience.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
And your experience makes you sad. I had rather have a
fool to make me merry than experience to make me sad—and
to travel for it, too.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
And your experience makes you sad. I had rather have a
fool to make me merry than experience to make me sad—and
to travel for it, too.
Enter ORLANDO

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Good day and happiness, dear Rosalind.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: Nay then, God be wi' you, an you talk in blank verse.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Farewell, Monsieur Traveler. Look you lisp and wear
strange suits, disable all the benefits of your own
country, be out of love with your nativity, and almost
chide God for making you that countenance you are, or I
will scarce think you have swam in a gondola.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:

country, be out of love with your nativity, and almost
chide God for making you that countenance you are, or I
will scarce think you have swam in a gondola.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Farewell, Monsieur Traveler. Look you lisp and wear

country, be out of love with your nativity, and almost
chide God for making you that countenance you are, or I
will scarce think you have swam in a gondola.
Exit JAQUES

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
[as Ganymede pretending to be ROSALIND] Why, how now,
Orlando, where have you been all this while? You a
lover? An you serve me such another trick, never come in
my sight more.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
[as Ganymede pretending to be ROSALIND] Why, how now,
Orlando, where have you been all this while? You a
lover? An you serve me such another trick, never come in
my sight more.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
[as Ganymede pretending to be ROSALIND] Why, how now,
Orlando, where have you been all this while? You a
lover? An you serve me such another trick, never come in

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
[as Ganymede pretending to be ROSALIND] Why, how now,
Orlando, where have you been all this while? You a
lover? An you serve me such another trick, never come in
my sight more.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: My fair Rosalind, I come within an hour of my promise.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Break an hour’s promise in love? He that will divide a
minute into a thousand parts and break but a part of the
thousand part of a minute in the affairs of love, it
may be said of him that Cupid hath clapped him o' th'
shoulder, but I’ll warrant him heart-whole.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Break an hour’s promise in love? He that will divide a
minute into a thousand parts and break but a part of the
thousand part of a minute in the affairs of love, it
may be said of him that Cupid hath clapped him o' th'
shoulder, but I’ll warrant him heart-whole.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Pardon me, dear Rosalind.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Nay, an you be so tardy, come no more in my sight. I
had as lief be wooed of a snail.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Nay, an you be so tardy, come no more in my sight. I
had as lief be wooed of a snail.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Of a snail?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Ay, of a snail, for though he comes slowly, he carries
his house on his head—a better jointure, I think, than
you make a woman. Besides, he brings his destiny with
him.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Ay, of a snail, for though he comes slowly, he carries
his house on his head—a better jointure, I think, than
you make a woman. Besides, he brings his destiny with
him.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: What’s that?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Why, horns, which such as you are fain to be beholding
to your wives for. But he comes armed in his fortune and
prevents the slander of his wife.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Why, horns, which such as you are fain to be beholding
to your wives for. But he comes armed in his fortune and
prevents the slander of his wife.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Virtue is no hornmaker, and my Rosalind is virtuous.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: And I am your Rosalind.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
[as Aliena] It pleases him to call you so, but he hath
a
Rosalind of a better leer than you.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Come, woo me, woo me, for now I am in a holiday humor,
and like enough to consent. What would you say to me
now, an I were your very, very Rosalind?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Come, woo me, woo me, for now I am in a holiday humor,
and like enough to consent. What would you say to me
now, an I were your very, very Rosalind?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: I would kiss before I spoke.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Nay, you were better speak first, and when you were
graveled for lack of matter, you might take occasion to
kiss. Very good orators, when they are out, they will
spit; and for lovers lacking—God warn us—matter, the
cleanliest shift is to kiss.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Nay, you were better speak first, and when you were
graveled for lack of matter, you might take occasion to
kiss. Very good orators, when they are out, they will
spit; and for lovers lacking—God warn us—matter, the
cleanliest shift is to kiss.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: How if the kiss be denied?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Then she puts you to entreaty, and there begins new
matter.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Who could be out, being before his beloved mistress?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Marry, that should you if I were your mistress, or I
should think my honesty ranker than my wit.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: What, of my suit?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Not out of your apparel, and yet out of your suit. Am
not I your Rosalind?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Not out of your apparel, and yet out of your suit. Am
not I your Rosalind?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
I take some joy to say you are because I would be
talking of her.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Well, in her person I say I will not have you.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Then, in mine own person I die.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
No, faith, die by attorney. The poor world is almost
six thousand years old, and in all this time there was
not any man died in his own person, videlicet, in a love
cause. Troilus had his brains dashed out with a Grecian
club, yet he did what he could to die before, and he is
one of the patterns of love. Leander, he would have
lived many a fair year though Hero had turned nun if it
had not been for a hot midsummer night, for, good youth,
he went but forth to wash him in the Hellespont and,
being taken with the cramp, was drowned; and the foolish
chroniclers of that age found it was Hero of Sestos.
But these are all lies. Men have died from time to time,
and worms have eaten them, but not for love.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
I would not have my right Rosalind of this mind, for I
protest her frown might kill me.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
By this hand, it will not kill a fly. But come; now I
will be your Rosalind in a more coming-on disposition,
and ask me what you will, I will grant it.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
By this hand, it will not kill a fly. But come; now I
will be your Rosalind in a more coming-on disposition,
and ask me what you will, I will grant it.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
By this hand, it will not kill a fly. But come; now I
will be your Rosalind in a more coming-on disposition,
and ask me what you will, I will grant it.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Then love me, Rosalind.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Yes, faith, will I, Fridays and Saturdays and all.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: And wilt thou have me?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Ay, and twenty such.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: What sayest thou?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Are you not good?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: I hope so.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Why then, can one desire too much of a good thing?—
Come, sister, you shall be the priest and marry us.—Give
me your hand, Orlando.—What do you say, sister?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Pray thee, marry us.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: I cannot say the words.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: You must begin “Will you, Orlando—”

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: Go to.—Will you, Orlando, have to wife this Rosalind?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: I will.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Ay, but when?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Why, now, as fast as she can marry us.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Then you must say “I take thee, Rosalind, for wife.”

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: I take thee, Rosalind, for wife.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I might ask you for your commission, but I do take
thee, Orlando, for my husband. There’s a girl goes
before the priest, and certainly a woman’s thought runs
before her actions.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I might ask you for your commission, but I do take
thee, Orlando, for my husband. There’s a girl goes
before the priest, and certainly a woman’s thought runs
before her actions.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I might ask you for your commission, but I do take
thee, Orlando, for my husband. There’s a girl goes
before the priest, and certainly a woman’s thought runs
before her actions.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: So do all thoughts. They are winged.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: So do all thoughts. They are winged.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Now tell me how long you would have her after you have
possessed her.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Forever and a day.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Say “a day” without the “ever.” No, no, Orlando, men
are April when they woo, December when they wed. Maids
are May when they are maids, but the sky changes when
they are wives. I will be more jealous of thee than a
Barbary cock- pigeon over his hen, more clamorous than a
parrot against rain, more newfangled than an ape, more
giddy in my desires than a monkey. I will weep for
nothing, like Diana in the fountain, and I will do that
when you are disposed to be merry. I will laugh like a
hyena, and that when thou art inclined to sleep.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: But will my Rosalind do so?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: By my life, she will do as I do.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Oh, but she is wise.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Or else she could not have the wit to do this. The
wiser, the waywarder. Make the doors upon a woman’s wit,
and it will out at the casement. Shut that, and ’twill
out at the keyhole. Stop that, ’twill fly with the smoke
out at the chimney.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
A man that had a wife with such a wit, he might say
“Wit, whither wilt?”

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Nay, you might keep that check for it, till you met
your wife’s wit going to your neighbor’s bed.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: And what wit could wit have to excuse that?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Marry, to say she came to seek you there. You shall
never take her without her answer unless you take her
without her tongue. Oh, that woman that cannot make her
fault her husband’s occasion, let her never nurse her
child herself, for she will breed it like a fool.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: For these two hours, Rosalind, I will leave thee.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Alas, dear love, I cannot lack thee two hours.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
I must attend the duke at dinner. By two o'clock I will
be with thee again.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
I must attend the duke at dinner. By two o'clock I will
be with thee again.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Ay, go your ways, go your ways. I knew what you would
prove. My friends told me as much, and I thought no
less. That flattering tongue of yours won me. 'Tis but
one cast away, and so, come, death. Two o'clock is your
hour?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Ay, sweet Rosalind.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
By my troth, and in good earnest, and so God mend me,
and by all pretty oaths that are not dangerous, if you
break one jot of your promise or come one minute behind
your hour, I will think you the most pathetical
break-promise and the most hollow lover and the most
unworthy of her you call Rosalind that may be chosen out
of the gross band of the unfaithful. Therefore beware
my censure, and keep your promise.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
With no less religion than if thou wert indeed my
Rosalind.
So, adieu.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:

Rosalind.
So, adieu.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Well, time is the old justice that examines all such
offenders, and let time try. Adieu.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Well, time is the old justice that examines all such
offenders, and let time try. Adieu.
Exit ORLANDO

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
You have simply misused our sex in your love-prate. We
must have your doublet and hose plucked over your head
and show the world what the bird hath done to her own
nest.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
You have simply misused our sex in your love-prate. We
must have your doublet and hose plucked over your head
and show the world what the bird hath done to her own
nest.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
O coz, coz, coz, my pretty little coz, that thou didst
know how many fathom deep I am in love. But it cannot
be sounded; my affection hath an unknown bottom, like
the Bay of Portugal.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
O coz, coz, coz, my pretty little coz, that thou didst
know how many fathom deep I am in love. But it cannot
be sounded; my affection hath an unknown bottom, like
the Bay of Portugal.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
Or rather bottomless, that as fast as you pour
affection in, it runs out.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
No, that same wicked bastard of Venus that was begot of
thought, conceived of spleen, and born of madness, that
blind rascally boy that abuses everyone’s eyes because
his own are out, let him be judge how deep I am in love.
I’ll tell thee, Aliena, I cannot be out of the sight of
Orlando. I’ll go find a shadow and sigh till he come.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: And I’ll sleep.
Exeunt
Enter JAQUES and LORDS, like foresters

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: Which is he that killed the deer?

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text: Sir, it was I.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
Let’s present him to the duke like a Roman conqueror.
And it would do well to set the deer’s horns upon his
head for a branch of victory.—Have you no song,
forester, for this purpose?

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
Let’s present him to the duke like a Roman conqueror.
And it would do well to set the deer’s horns upon his
head for a branch of victory.—Have you no song,
forester, for this purpose?

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
Let’s present him to the duke like a Roman conqueror.
And it would do well to set the deer’s horns upon his
head for a branch of victory.—Have you no song,
forester, for this purpose?

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text: Yes, sir.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
Sing it. 'Tis no matter how it be in tune, so it make
noise enough.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
Sing it. 'Tis no matter how it be in tune, so it make
noise enough.

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text:
[sings]
What shall he have that killed the deer?
His leather skin and horns to wear.
Then sing him home.
(The rest shall bear this burden.)
Take thou no scorn to wear the horn.
It was a crest ere thou wast born.
Thy father’s father wore it,
And thy father bore it.
The horn, the horn, the lusty horn
Is not a thing to laugh to scorn.

Enter ROSALIND and CELIA

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
How say you now? Is it not past two o'clock? And here
much Orlando.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
How say you now? Is it not past two o'clock? And here
much Orlando.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
How say you now? Is it not past two o'clock? And here
much Orlando.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
I warrant you, with pure love and troubled brain he
hath ta'en his bow and arrows and is gone forth to
sleep.
Enter SILVIUS

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: Look who comes here.

[To ROSALIND] My errand is to you, fair youth.
My gentle Phoebe did bid me give you this.
I know not the contents, but as I guess
By the stern brow and waspish action
Which she did use as she was writing of it,
It bears an angry tenor. Pardon me.
I am but as a guiltless messenger.
[Gives the letter]

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
[Examines the letter as Ganymede] Patience herself
would startle at this letter
And play the swaggerer. Bear this, bear all.
She says I am not fair, that I lack manners.
She calls me proud, and that she could not love me
Were man as rare as phoenix. 'Od’s my will,
Her love is not the hare that I do hunt.
Why writes she so to me? Well, shepherd, well,
This is a letter of your own device.

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text:
No, I protest, I know not the contents.
Phoebe did write it.

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text:
No, I protest, I know not the contents.
Phoebe did write it.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Come, come, you are a fool,
And turned into the extremity of love.
I saw her hand. She has a leathern hand,
A freestone-colored hand. I verily did think
That her old gloves were on, but ’twas her hands.
She has a huswife’s hand—but that’s no matter.
I say she never did invent this letter.
This is a man’s invention, and his hand.

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text: Sure it is hers.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Why, ’tis a boisterous and a cruel style,
A style for challengers. Why, she defies me
Like Turk to Christian. Women’s gentle brain
Could not drop forth such giant-rude invention,
Such Ethiop words, blacker in their effect
Than in their countenance. Will you hear the letter?

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text:
So please you, for I never heard it yet,
Yet heard too much of Phoebe’s cruelty.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
She Phoebes me. Mark how the tyrant writes.
[reads] Art thou god to shepherd turned,
That a maiden’s heart hath burned?
Can a woman rail thus?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
She Phoebes me. Mark how the tyrant writes.
[reads] Art thou god to shepherd turned,
That a maiden’s heart hath burned?
Can a woman rail thus?

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text: Call you this railing?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
[reads]
Why, thy godhead laid apart,
Warr’st thou with a woman’s heart?
Did you ever hear such railing?
Whiles the eye of man did woo me,
That could do no vengeance to me.
Meaning me a beast.
If the scorn of your bright eyne
Have power to raise such love in mine,
Alack, in me what strange effect
Would they work in mild aspect?
Whiles you chid me, I did love.
How then might your prayers move?
He that brings this love to thee
Little knows this love in me,
And by him seal up thy mind
Whether that thy youth and kind
Will the faithful offer take
Of me, and all that I can make,
Or else by him my love deny,
And then I’ll study how to die.

Have power to raise such love in mine,
Alack, in me what strange effect
Would they work in mild aspect?
Whiles you chid me, I did love.
How then might your prayers move?
He that brings this love to thee
Little knows this love in me,
And by him seal up thy mind
Whether that thy youth and kind
Will the faithful offer take
Of me, and all that I can make,
Or else by him my love deny,
And then I’ll study how to die.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
[reads]
Why, thy godhead laid apart,
Warr’st thou with a woman’s heart?
Did you ever hear such railing?
Whiles the eye of man did woo me,
That could do no vengeance to me.
Meaning me a beast.
If the scorn of your bright eyne
Have power to raise such love in mine,
Alack, in me what strange effect
Would they work in mild aspect?
Whiles you chid me, I did love.
How then might your prayers move?
He that brings this love to thee
Little knows this love in me,
And by him seal up thy mind
Whether that thy youth and kind
Will the faithful offer take
Of me, and all that I can make,
Or else by him my love deny,
And then I’ll study how to die.

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text: Call you this chiding?

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: [As Aliena] Alas, poor shepherd.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Do you pity him? No, he deserves no pity.— Wilt thou
love such a woman? What, to make thee an instrument and
play false strains upon thee? Not to be endured. Well,
go your way to her, for I see love hath made thee a tame
snake, and say this to her: that if she love me, I
charge her to love thee; if she will not, I will never
have her unless thou entreat for her. If you be a true
lover, hence and not a word, for here comes more
company.

Enter OLIVER

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Good morrow, fair ones. Pray you, if you know,
Where in the purlieus of this forest stands
A sheepcote fenced about with olive trees?

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Good morrow, fair ones. Pray you, if you know,
Where in the purlieus of this forest stands
A sheepcote fenced about with olive trees?

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
[as Aliena] West of this place, down in the neighbor
bottom,
The rank of osiers by the murmuring stream
Left on your right hand brings you to the place.
But at this hour the house doth keep itself.
There’s none within.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:

bottom,

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
If that an eye may profit by a tongue,
Then should I know you by description.
Such garments, and such years. “The boy is fair,
Of female favor, and bestows himself
Like a ripe sister; the woman low
And browner than her brother.” Are not you
The owner of the house I did inquire for?

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: It is no boast, being asked, to say we are.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Orlando doth commend him to you both,
And to that youth he calls his Rosalind
He sends this bloody napkin. Are you he?

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Orlando doth commend him to you both,
And to that youth he calls his Rosalind
He sends this bloody napkin. Are you he?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: [as Ganymede] I am. What must we understand by this?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: [as Ganymede] I am. What must we understand by this?

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Some of my shame, if you will know of me
What man I am, and how, and why, and where
This handkercher was stained.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: I pray you, tell it.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
When last the young Orlando parted from you,
He left a promise to return again
Within an hour, and pacing through the forest,
Chewing the food of sweet and bitter fancy,
Lo, what befell. He threw his eye aside—
And mark what object did present itself:
Under an old oak, whose boughs were mossed with age
And high top bald with dry antiquity,
A wretched, ragged man, o'ergrown with hair,
Lay sleeping on his back. About his neck
A green and gilded snake had wreathed itself,
Who with her head, nimble in threats, approached
The opening of his mouth. But suddenly,
Seeing Orlando, it unlinked itself
And, with indented glides, did slip away
Into a bush, under which bush’s shade
A lioness, with udders all drawn dry,
Lay couching, head on ground, with catlike watch
When that the sleeping man should stir—for ’tis
The royal disposition of that beast
To prey on nothing that doth seem as dead.
This seen, Orlando did approach the man
And found it was his brother, his elder brother.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
Oh, I have heard him speak of that same brother,
And he did render him the most unnatural
That lived amongst men.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
And well he might so do,
For well I know he was unnatural.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
And well he might so do,
For well I know he was unnatural.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
But to Orlando: did he leave him there,
Food to the sucked and hungry lioness?

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Twice did he turn his back and purposed so,
But kindness, nobler ever than revenge,
And nature, stronger than his just occasion,
Made him give battle to the lioness,
Who quickly fell before him; in which hurtling,
From miserable slumber I awaked.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: Are you his brother?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Was ’t you he rescued?

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: Was ’t you that did so oft contrive to kill him?

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
'Twas I, but ’tis not I. I do not shame
To tell you what I was, since my conversion
So sweetly tastes, being the thing I am.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
'Twas I, but ’tis not I. I do not shame
To tell you what I was, since my conversion
So sweetly tastes, being the thing I am.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: But for the bloody napkin?

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
By and by.
When from the first to last betwixt us two
Tears our recountments had most kindly bathed—
As how I came into that desert place—
In brief, he led me to the gentle duke,
Who gave me fresh array and entertainment,
Committing me unto my brother’s love,
Who led me instantly unto his cave,
There stripped himself, and here upon his arm
The lioness had torn some flesh away,
Which all this while had bled. And now he fainted,
And cried in fainting upon Rosalind.
Brief, I recovered him, bound up his wound,
And after some small space, being strong at heart,
He sent me hither, stranger as I am,
To tell this story, that you might excuse
His broken promise, and to give this napkin
Dyed in his blood unto the shepherd youth
That he in sport doth call his Rosalind.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: Why, how now, Ganymede, sweet Ganymede?

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text: Many will swoon when they do look on blood.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text: There is more in it.—Cousin Ganymede.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text: Look, he recovers.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: I would I were at home.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
We’ll lead you thither.
—I pray you, will you take him by the arm?

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Be of good cheer, youth. You a man? You lack a man’s
heart.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Be of good cheer, youth. You a man? You lack a man’s
heart.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Be of good cheer, youth. You a man? You lack a man’s
heart.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I do so, I confess it. Ah, sirrah, a body would think
this was well-counterfeited. I pray you tell your
brother how well I counterfeited. Heigh-ho.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:

this was well-counterfeited. I pray you tell your
brother how well I counterfeited. Heigh-ho.
