sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
This was not counterfeit. There is too great testimony
in your complexion that it was a passion of earnest.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
This was not counterfeit. There is too great testimony
in your complexion that it was a passion of earnest.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Counterfeit, I assure you.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Well then, take a good heart and counterfeit to be a
man.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
So I do. But i' faith, I should have been a woman by
right.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
So I do. But i' faith, I should have been a woman by
right.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
Come, you look paler and paler. Pray you, draw
homewards.—Good sir, go with us.

sentences.speaker: CELIA
sentences.text:
Come, you look paler and paler. Pray you, draw
homewards.—Good sir, go with us.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
That will I, for I must bear answer back
How you excuse my brother, Rosalind.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I shall devise something. But I pray you commend my
counterfeiting to him. Will you go?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:

Enter TOUCHSTONE and AUDREY

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text: We shall find a time, Audrey. Patience, gentle Audrey.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text: We shall find a time, Audrey. Patience, gentle Audrey.

sentences.speaker: AUDREY
sentences.text:
Faith, the priest was good enough, for all the old
gentleman’s saying.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
A most wicked Sir Oliver, Audrey, a most vile Martext.
But, Audrey, there is a youth here in the forest lays
claim to you.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
A most wicked Sir Oliver, Audrey, a most vile Martext.
But, Audrey, there is a youth here in the forest lays
claim to you.

sentences.speaker: AUDREY
sentences.text:
Ay, I know who ’tis. He hath no interest in me in the
world.

sentences.speaker: AUDREY
sentences.text:
Ay, I know who ’tis. He hath no interest in me in the
world.
Enter WILLIAM

sentences.speaker: AUDREY
sentences.text: Here comes the man you mean.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
It is meat and drink to me to see a clown. By my
troth, we that have good wits have much to answer for.
We shall be flouting. We cannot hold.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: WILLIAM
sentences.text: Good ev'n, Audrey.

sentences.speaker: AUDREY
sentences.text: God gi' good ev'n, William.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAM
sentences.text: And good ev'n to you, sir.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Good ev'n, gentle friend. Cover thy head, cover thy
head.
Nay, prithee, be covered. How old are you, friend?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Good ev'n, gentle friend. Cover thy head, cover thy
head.
Nay, prithee, be covered. How old are you, friend?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Good ev'n, gentle friend. Cover thy head, cover thy
head.
Nay, prithee, be covered. How old are you, friend?

sentences.speaker: WILLIAM
sentences.text: Five-and-twenty, sir.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text: A ripe age. Is thy name William?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text: A ripe age. Is thy name William?

sentences.speaker: WILLIAM
sentences.text: William, sir.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text: A fair name. Wast born i' th' forest here?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text: A fair name. Wast born i' th' forest here?

sentences.speaker: WILLIAM
sentences.text: Ay, sir, I thank God.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text: “Thank God.” A good answer. Art rich?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text: “Thank God.” A good answer. Art rich?

sentences.speaker: WILLIAM
sentences.text: 'Faith, sir, so-so.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
“So-so” is good, very good, very excellent good. And
yet it is not: it is but so-so. Art thou wise?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
“So-so” is good, very good, very excellent good. And
yet it is not: it is but so-so. Art thou wise?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
“So-so” is good, very good, very excellent good. And
yet it is not: it is but so-so. Art thou wise?

sentences.speaker: WILLIAM
sentences.text: Ay, sir, I have a pretty wit.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Why, thou sayst well. I do now remember a saying: “The
fool doth think he is wise, but the wise man knows
himself to be a fool.” The heathen philosopher, when he
had a desire to eat a grape, would open his lips when he
put it into his mouth, meaning thereby that grapes were
made to eat and lips to open. You do love this maid?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: WILLIAM
sentences.text: I do, sir.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text: Give me your hand. Art thou learned?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text: Give me your hand. Art thou learned?

sentences.speaker: WILLIAM
sentences.text: No, sir.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Then learn this of me: to have is to have. For it is a
figure in rhetoric that drink, being poured out of a cup
into a glass, by filling the one doth empty the other.
For all your writers do consent that ipse is “he.” Now,
you are not ipse, for I am he.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:

figure in rhetoric that drink, being poured out of a cup
into a glass, by filling the one doth empty the other.
For all your writers do consent that ipse is “he.” Now,
you are not ipse, for I am he.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Then learn this of me: to have is to have. For it is a
figure in rhetoric that drink, being poured out of a cup
into a glass, by filling the one doth empty the other.
For all your writers do consent that ipse is “he.” Now,
you are not ipse, for I am he.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAM
sentences.text: Which he, sir?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
He, sir, that must marry this woman. Therefore, you
clown, abandon—which is, in the vulgar, “leave”—the
society—which in the boorish is “company”—of this
female—which in the common is “woman” ; which together
is, abandon the society of this female, or, clown, thou
perishest; or, to thy better understanding, diest; or,
to wit, I kill thee, make thee away, translate thy life
into death, thy liberty into bondage. I will deal in
poison with thee, or in bastinado, or in steel. I will
bandy with thee in faction. I will o'errun thee with
policy. I will kill thee a hundred and fifty ways.
Therefore tremble and depart.

sentences.speaker: AUDREY

sentences.speaker: WILLIAM
sentences.text: God rest you merry, sir.
Exit
Enter CORIN

sentences.speaker: CORIN
sentences.text: Our master and mistress seeks you. Come away, away.

sentences.speaker: CORIN
sentences.text: Our master and mistress seeks you. Come away, away.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text: Trip, Audrey, trip, Audrey.—I attend, I attend.

Enter ORLANDO and OLIVER

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
Is ’t possible that on so little acquaintance you
should like her? That, but seeing, you should love her?
And loving, woo? And wooing, she should grant? And will
you persevere to enjoy her?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:

And loving, woo? And wooing, she should grant? And will
you persevere to enjoy her?

sentences.text:
Is ’t possible that on so little acquaintance you

And loving, woo? And wooing, she should grant? And will

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
Is ’t possible that on so little acquaintance you
should like her? That, but seeing, you should love her?
And loving, woo? And wooing, she should grant? And will
you persevere to enjoy her?

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text:
Neither call the giddiness of it in question, the
poverty of her, the small acquaintance, my sudden
wooing, nor her sudden consenting, but say with me “I
love Aliena”; say with her that she loves me; consent
with both that we may enjoy each other. It shall be to
your good, for my father’s house and all the revenue
that was old Sir Rowland’s will I estate upon you, and
here live and die a shepherd.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
You have my consent. Let your wedding be tomorrow.
Thither will I invite the duke and all’s contented
followers.
Go you and prepare Aliena, for look you, here comes my
Rosalind.

Enter ROSALIND

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: [As Ganymede] God save you, brother.

sentences.speaker: OLIVER
sentences.text: And you, fair sister.
Exit

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
O my dear Orlando, how it grieves me to see thee wear
thy heart in a scarf.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: It is my arm.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I thought thy heart had been wounded with the claws of
a lion.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Wounded it is, but with the eyes of a lady.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Did your brother tell you how I counterfeited to swoon
when he showed me your handkercher?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Ay, and greater wonders than that.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Oh, I know where you are. Nay, ’tis true. There was
never anything so sudden but the fight of two rams and
Caesar’s thrasonical brag of “I came, saw, and
overcame.” For your brother and my sister no sooner met
but they looked, no sooner looked but they loved, no
sooner loved but they sighed, no sooner sighed but they
asked one another the reason, no sooner knew the reason
but they sought the remedy; and in these degrees have
they made a pair of stairs to marriage, which they will
climb incontinent, or else be incontinent before
marriage. They are in the very wrath of love, and they
will together. Clubs cannot part them.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
They shall be married tomorrow, and I will bid the duke
to the nuptial. But Oh, how bitter a thing it is to
look into happiness through another man’s eyes. By so
much the more shall I tomorrow be at the height of
heart-heaviness, by how much I shall think my brother
happy in having what he wishes for.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:

to the nuptial. But Oh, how bitter a thing it is to
look into happiness through another man’s eyes. By so
much the more shall I tomorrow be at the height of
heart-heaviness, by how much I shall think my brother
happy in having what he wishes for.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
They shall be married tomorrow, and I will bid the duke
to the nuptial. But Oh, how bitter a thing it is to
look into happiness through another man’s eyes. By so
much the more shall I tomorrow be at the height of
heart-heaviness, by how much I shall think my brother
happy in having what he wishes for.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Why, then, tomorrow I cannot serve your turn for
Rosalind?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: I can live no longer by thinking.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I will weary you then no longer with idle talking. Know
of me then—for now I speak to some purpose—that I know
you are a gentleman of good conceit. I speak not this
that you should bear a good opinion of my knowledge,
insomuch I say I know you are. Neither do I labor for a
greater esteem than may in some little measure draw a
belief from you to do yourself good, and not to grace
me. Believe then, if you please, that I can do strange
things. I have, since I was three year old, conversed
with a magician, most profound in his art and yet not
damnable. If you do love Rosalind so near the heart as
your gesture cries it out, when your brother marries
Aliena shall you marry her. I know into what straits of
fortune she is driven, and it is not impossible to me,
if it appear not inconvenient to you, to set her before
your eyes tomorrow, human as she is, and without any
danger.

damnable. If you do love Rosalind so near the heart as
your gesture cries it out, when your brother marries
Aliena shall you marry her. I know into what straits of
fortune she is driven, and it is not impossible to me,
if it appear not inconvenient to you, to set her before
your eyes tomorrow, human as she is, and without any
danger.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I will weary you then no longer with idle talking. Know
of me then—for now I speak to some purpose—that I know
you are a gentleman of good conceit. I speak not this
that you should bear a good opinion of my knowledge,
insomuch I say I know you are. Neither do I labor for a
greater esteem than may in some little measure draw a
belief from you to do yourself good, and not to grace
me. Believe then, if you please, that I can do strange
things. I have, since I was three year old, conversed
with a magician, most profound in his art and yet not
damnable. If you do love Rosalind so near the heart as
your gesture cries it out, when your brother marries

fortune she is driven, and it is not impossible to me,
if it appear not inconvenient to you, to set her before
your eyes tomorrow, human as she is, and without any
danger.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Speak’st thou in sober meanings?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
By my life I do, which I tender dearly, though I say I
am a magician. Therefore put you in your best array, bid
your friends; for if you will be married tomorrow, you
shall, and to Rosalind, if you will.

sentences.text:

Enter SILVIUS and PHOEBE

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Look, here comes a lover of mine and a lover of hers.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text:
Youth, you have done me much ungentleness
To show the letter that I writ to you.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I care not if I have. It is my study
To seem despiteful and ungentle to you.
You are there followed by a faithful shepherd.
Look upon him, love him; he worships you.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I care not if I have. It is my study
To seem despiteful and ungentle to you.
You are there followed by a faithful shepherd.
Look upon him, love him; he worships you.

sentences.text:

To seem despiteful and ungentle to you.
You are there followed by a faithful shepherd.
Look upon him, love him; he worships you.

sentences.text:

To seem despiteful and ungentle to you.
You are there followed by a faithful shepherd.
Look upon him, love him; he worships you.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I care not if I have. It is my study
To seem despiteful and ungentle to you.
You are there followed by a faithful shepherd.
Look upon him, love him; he worships you.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text: Good shepherd, tell this youth what ’tis to love.

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text:
It is to be all made of sighs and tears,
And so am I for Phoebe.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text: And I for Ganymede.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: And I for Rosalind.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: And I for no woman.

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text:
It is to be all made of faith and service,
And so am I for Phoebe.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text: And I for Ganymede.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: And I for Rosalind.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: And I for no woman.

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text:
It is to be all made of fantasy,
All made of passion and all made of wishes,
All adoration, duty, and observance,
All humbleness, all patience and impatience,
All purity, all trial, all observance,
And so am I for Phoebe.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text: And so am I for Ganymede.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: And so am I for Rosalind.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: And so am I for no woman.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text: If this be so, why blame you me to love you?

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text: If this be so, why blame you me to love you?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: If this be so, why blame you me to love you?

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: Why do you speak, too, “Why blame you me to love you?”?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: To her that is not here, nor doth not hear.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
Pray you, no more of this. 'Tis like the howling of
Irish wolves against the moon. [to SILVIUS] I will help
you, if I can. [to PHOEBE] I would love you if I
could.— Tomorrow meet me all together. [to PHOEBE] I
will marry you if ever I marry woman, and I’ll be
married tomorrow. [to ORLANDO] I will satisfy you if
ever I satisfy man, and you shall be married tomorrow.
[to SILVIUS] I will content you, if what pleases you
contents you, and you shall be married tomorrow. [to
ORLANDO] As you love Rosalind, meet. [to SILVIUS] As
you love Phoebe, meet.— And as I love no woman, I’ll
meet. So fare you well. I have left you commands.

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text: I’ll not fail, if I live.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text: Nor I.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: Nor I.
Exeunt
Enter TOUCHSTONE and AUDREY.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Tomorrow is the joyful day, Audrey. Tomorrow will we be
married.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Tomorrow is the joyful day, Audrey. Tomorrow will we be
married.

sentences.speaker: AUDREY
sentences.text:
I do desire it with all my heart, and I hope it is no
dishonest desire to desire to be a woman of the world.
Enter two PAGES

sentences.speaker: AUDREY
sentences.text: Here comes two of the banished duke’s pages.

sentences.speaker: FIRST PAGE
sentences.text: Well met, honest gentleman.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text: By my troth, well met. Come, sit, sit, and a song.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text: By my troth, well met. Come, sit, sit, and a song.

sentences.speaker: SECOND PAGE
sentences.text: We are for you. Sit i' th' middle.

sentences.speaker: SECOND PAGE
sentences.text: We are for you. Sit i' th' middle.

sentences.speaker: FIRST PAGE
sentences.text:
Shall we clap into ’t roundly, without hawking or
spitting or saying we are hoarse, which are the only
prologues to a bad voice?

sentences.speaker: SECOND PAGE
sentences.text:
I' faith, i' faith, and both in a tune like two gypsies
on a horse.

sentences.speaker: PAGES
sentences.text:
[sing]
It was a lover and his lass,
With a hey, and a ho, and a hey-nonny-no,
That o'er the green cornfield did pass
In springtime, the only pretty ring time,
When birds do sing, Hey ding a ding, ding.
Sweet lovers love the spring.
Between the acres of the rye,
With a hey, and a ho, and a hey-nonny-no,
These pretty country folks would lie
In springtime, the only pretty ring time,
When birds do sing, Hey ding a ding, ding.
Sweet lovers love the spring.
This carol they began that hour,
With a hey, and a ho, and a hey-nonny-no,
How that a life was but a flower
In springtime, the only pretty ring time,
When birds do sing, Hey ding a ding, ding.
Sweet lovers love the spring.
And therefore take the present time,
With a hey, and a ho, and a hhey-nonny-no,
For love is crownèd with the prime
In springtime, the only pretty ring time,
When birds do sing, Hey ding a ding, ding.
Sweet lovers love the spring.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Truly, young gentlemen, though there was no great
matter in the ditty, yet the note was very untunable.

sentences.speaker: FIRST PAGE
sentences.text:
You are deceived, sir. We kept time. We lost not our
time.

sentences.speaker: FIRST PAGE
sentences.text:
You are deceived, sir. We kept time. We lost not our
time.

sentences.speaker: FIRST PAGE
sentences.text:
You are deceived, sir. We kept time. We lost not our
time.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
By my troth, yes. I count it but time lost to hear such
a foolish song. God be wi' you, and God mend your
voices.— Come, Audrey.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
By my troth, yes. I count it but time lost to hear such
a foolish song. God be wi' you, and God mend your
voices.— Come, Audrey.

Exeunt
Enter DUKE SENIOR, AMIENS, JAQUES, ORLANDO, OLIVER, and CELIA

sentences.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
sentences.text:
Dost thou believe, Orlando, that the boy
Can do all this that he hath promisèd?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
I sometimes do believe and sometimes do not,
As those that fear they hope, and know they fear.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
I sometimes do believe and sometimes do not,
As those that fear they hope, and know they fear.
Enter ROSALIND, SILVIUS, and PHOEBE

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
[as Ganymede] Patience once more whiles our compact is
urged.
[to DUKE SENIOR] You say, if I bring in your Rosalind,
You will bestow her on Orlando here?

sentences.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
sentences.text: That would I, had I kingdoms to give with her.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
[to ORLANDO] And you say you will have her when I bring
her?

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: That would I, were I of all kingdoms king.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text: [to PHOEBE] You say you’ll marry me if I be willing?

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text: That will I, should I die the hour after.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
But if you do refuse to marry me,
You’ll give yourself to this most faithful shepherd?

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text: So is the bargain.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
[to SILVIUS] You say that you’ll have Phoebe if she
will?

sentences.speaker: SILVIUS
sentences.text: Though to have her and death were both one thing.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
I have promised to make all this matter even.
Keep you your word, O duke, to give your daughter,
—You yours, Orlando, to receive his daughter.
—Keep your word, Phoebe, that you’ll marry me
Or else, refusing me, to wed this shepherd.
—Keep your word, Silvius, that you’ll marry her
If she refuse me. And from hence I go
To make these doubts all even.
Exeunt ROSALIND and CELIA

sentences.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
sentences.text:
I do remember in this shepherd boy
Some lively touches of my daughter’s favor.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:
My lord, the first time that I ever saw him
Methought he was a brother to your daughter.
But, my good lord, this boy is forest-born
And hath been tutored in the rudiments
Of many desperate studies by his uncle,
Whom he reports to be a great magician
Obscurèd in the circle of this forest.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text:

Enter TOUCHSTONE and AUDREY

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
There is sure another flood toward, and these couples
are coming to the ark. Here comes a pair of very strange
beasts, which in all tongues are called fools.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
There is sure another flood toward, and these couples
are coming to the ark. Here comes a pair of very strange
beasts, which in all tongues are called fools.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text: Salutation and greeting to you all.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
Good my lord, bid him welcome. This is the
motley-minded gentleman that I have so often met in the
forest. He hath been a courtier, he swears.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
Good my lord, bid him welcome. This is the
motley-minded gentleman that I have so often met in the
forest. He hath been a courtier, he swears.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
If any man doubt that, let him put me to my purgation.
I have trod a measure. I have flattered a lady. I have
been politic with my friend, smooth with mine enemy. I
have undone three tailors. I have had four quarrels, and
like to have fought one.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: And how was that ta'en up?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Faith, we met and found the quarrel was upon the
seventh cause.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: How “seventh cause?”—Good my lord, like this fellow.

sentences.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
sentences.text: I like him very well.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
God 'ild you, sir. I desire you of the like. I press in
here, sir, amongst the rest of the country copulatives,
to swear and to forswear, according as marriage binds
and blood breaks. A poor virgin, sir, an ill-favored
thing, sir, but mine own. A poor humor of mine, sir, to
take that that no man else will. Rich honesty dwells
like a miser, sir, in a poor house, as your pearl in
your foul oyster.

sentences.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
sentences.text: By my faith, he is very swift and sententious.

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
According to the fool’s bolt, sir, and such dulcet
diseases.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
But for the seventh cause. How did you find the quarrel
on the seventh cause?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
Upon a lie seven times removed.— Bear your body more
seeming, Audrey.— As thus, sir: I did dislike the cut of
a certain courtier’s beard. He sent me word if I said
his beard was not cut well, he was in the mind it was.
This is called “the retort courteous.” If I sent him word
again it was not well cut, he would send me word he cut
it to please himself. This is called “the quip modest.”
If again it was not well cut, he disabled my judgment.
This is called “the reply churlish.” If again it was not
well cut, he would answer I spake not true. This is
called “the reproof valiant.” If again it was not well
cut, he would say I lie. This is called “the
countercheck quarrelsome,” and so to “the lie
circumstantial” and “the lie direct.”

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: And how oft did you say his beard was not well cut?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
I durst go no further than the lie circumstantial, nor
he durst not give me the lie direct, and so we measured
swords and parted.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text: Can you nominate in order now the degrees of the lie?

sentences.speaker: TOUCHSTONE
sentences.text:
O sir, we quarrel in print, by the book, as you have
books for good manners. I will name you the degrees: the
first, “the retort courteous;” the second, “the quip
modest;” the third, “the reply churlish;” the fourth,
“the reproof valiant;” the fifth, “the countercheque
quarrelsome;” the sixth, “the lie with circumstance;”
the seventh, “the lie direct.” All these you may avoid
but the lie direct, and you may avoid that, too, with an
“if.” I knew when seven justices could not take up a
quarrel, but when the parties were met themselves, one
of them thought but of an “if,” as: “If you said so,
then I said so.” And they shook hands and swore
brothers. Your “if” is the only peacemaker: much virtue
in “if.”

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
Is not this a rare fellow, my lord? He’s as good at
anything and yet a fool.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
Is not this a rare fellow, my lord? He’s as good at
anything and yet a fool.

sentences.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
sentences.text:
He uses his folly like a stalking-horse, and under the
presentation of that he shoots his wit.
Enter HYMEN, ROSALIND, and CELIA. Soft music
Enter HYMEN, ROSALIND, and CELIA. Soft music

sentences.speaker: HYMEN
sentences.text:
Then is there mirth in heaven
When earthly things, made even,
Atone together.
Good duke, receive thy daughter.
Hymen from heaven brought her,
Yea, brought her hither,
That thou mightst join her hand with his
Whose heart within her bosom is.

sentences.speaker: HYMEN
sentences.text:
Then is there mirth in heaven
When earthly things, made even,
Atone together.
Good duke, receive thy daughter.
Hymen from heaven brought her,
Yea, brought her hither,
That thou mightst join her hand with his
Whose heart within her bosom is.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
[to DUKE SENIOR] To you I give myself, for I am yours.
[to ORLANDO] To you I give myself, for I am yours.

sentences.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
sentences.text: If there be truth in sight, you are my daughter.

sentences.speaker: ORLANDO
sentences.text: If there be truth in sight, you are my Rosalind.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text:
If sight and shape be true,
Why then, my love adieu.

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
[to DUKE SENIOR] I’ll have no father, if you be not he.
[to ORLANDO] I’ll have no husband, if you be not he,
[to PHOEBE] Nor ne'er wed woman, if you be not she.

sentences.speaker: HYMEN
sentences.text:
Peace, ho! I bar confusion.
'Tis I must make conclusion
Of these most strange events.
Here’s eight that must take hands
To join in Hymen’s bands,
If truth holds true contents.
[to ORLANDO and ROSALIND]
You and you no cross shall part.
[to OLIVER and CELIA]
You and you are heart in heart.
[to PHOEBE]
You to his love must accord
Or have a woman to your lord.
[to TOUCHSTONE and AUDREY]
You and you are sure together
As the winter to foul weather.
[to all]
Whiles a wedlock hymn we sing,
Feed yourselves with questioning,
That reason wonder may diminish
How thus we met, and these things finish.
[sings]
Wedding is great Juno’s crown,
O blessèd bond of board and bed.
'Tis Hymen peoples every town.
High wedlock then be honorèd.
Honor, high honor, and renown,
To Hymen, god of every town.

[to all]
Whiles a wedlock hymn we sing,
Feed yourselves with questioning,
That reason wonder may diminish
How thus we met, and these things finish.
[sings]
Wedding is great Juno’s crown,
O blessèd bond of board and bed.
'Tis Hymen peoples every town.
High wedlock then be honorèd.
Honor, high honor, and renown,
To Hymen, god of every town.

sentences.speaker: HYMEN
sentences.text:
Peace, ho! I bar confusion.
'Tis I must make conclusion
Of these most strange events.
Here’s eight that must take hands
To join in Hymen’s bands,
If truth holds true contents.
[to ORLANDO and ROSALIND]
You and you no cross shall part.
[to OLIVER and CELIA]
You and you are heart in heart.
[to PHOEBE]
You to his love must accord
Or have a woman to your lord.
[to TOUCHSTONE and AUDREY]
You and you are sure together
As the winter to foul weather.
[to all]
Whiles a wedlock hymn we sing,
Feed yourselves with questioning,
That reason wonder may diminish
How thus we met, and these things finish.
[sings]
Wedding is great Juno’s crown,
O blessèd bond of board and bed.
'Tis Hymen peoples every town.
High wedlock then be honorèd.
Honor, high honor, and renown,
To Hymen, god of every town.

sentences.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
sentences.text:
O my dear niece, welcome thou art to me,
Even daughter, welcome in no less degree.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text:
I will not eat my word. Now thou art mine,
Thy faith my fancy to thee doth combine.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text:
I will not eat my word. Now thou art mine,
Thy faith my fancy to thee doth combine.

sentences.speaker: PHOEBE
sentences.text:
I will not eat my word. Now thou art mine,
Thy faith my fancy to thee doth combine.
Enter JAQUES DE BOYS

sentences.speaker: JAQUES DE BOYS
sentences.text:
Let me have audience for a word or two.
I am the second son of old Sir Rowland,
That bring these tidings to this fair assembly.
Duke Frederick, hearing how that every day
Men of great worth resorted to this forest,
Addressed a mighty power, which were on foot
In his own conduct, purposely to take
His brother here and put him to the sword.
And to the skirts of this wild wood he came,
Where, meeting with an old religious man,
After some question with him, was converted
Both from his enterprise and from the world,
His crown bequeathing to his banished brother,
And all their lands restored to them again
That were with him exiled. This to be true
I do engage my life.

sentences.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
sentences.text:
Welcome, young man.
Thou offer’st fairly to thy brothers' wedding:
To one his lands withheld, and to the other
A land itself at large, a potent dukedom.
—First, in this forest let us do those ends
That here were well begun and well begot,
And, after, every of this happy number
That have endured shrewd days and nights with us
Shall share the good of our returnèd fortune
According to the measure of their states.
Meantime, forget this new-fall'n dignity,
And fall into our rustic revelry.
—Play, music.— And you brides and bridegrooms all,
With measure heaped in joy to th' measures fall.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
Sir, by your patience: if I heard you rightly,
The duke hath put on a religious life
And thrown into neglect the pompous court.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES DE BOYS
sentences.text: He hath.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
To him will I. Out of these convertites
There is much matter to be heard and learned.
[to DUKE SENIOR]
You to your former honor I bequeath;
Your patience and your virtue well deserves it.
[to ORLANDO]
You to a love that your true faith doth merit.
[to OLIVER]
You to your land, and love, and great allies.
[to SILVIUS]
You to a long and well-deservèd bed.
[to TOUCHSTONE]
And you to wrangling, for thy loving voyage
Is but for two months victualled.— So to your pleasures.
I am for other than for dancing measures.

sentences.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
sentences.text: Stay, Jaques, stay.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
To see no pastime I. What you would have
I’ll stay to know at your abandoned cave.

sentences.speaker: JAQUES
sentences.text:
To see no pastime I. What you would have
I’ll stay to know at your abandoned cave.
Exit

sentences.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
sentences.text:
Proceed, proceed. We’ll so begin these rites
As we do trust they’ll end, in true delights.

sentences.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
sentences.text:
Proceed, proceed. We’ll so begin these rites
As we do trust they’ll end, in true delights.

sentences.speaker: DUKE SENIOR
sentences.text:
Proceed, proceed. We’ll so begin these rites
As we do trust they’ll end, in true delights.
Exeunt all but ROSALIND

sentences.speaker: ROSALIND
sentences.text:
It is not the fashion to see the lady the epilogue, but
it is no more unhandsome than to see the lord the
prologue. If it be true that good wine needs no bush,
’tis true that a good play needs no epilogue. Yet to
good wine they do use good bushes, and good plays prove
the better by the help of good epilogues. What a case am
I in, then, that am neither a good epilogue nor cannot
insinuate with you in the behalf of a good play. I am
not furnished like a beggar; therefore to beg will not
become me. My way is to conjure you, and I’ll begin with
the women. I charge you, O women, for the love you bear
to men, to like as much of this play as please you. And
I charge you, O men, for the love you bear to women—as
I perceive by your simpering, none of you hates
them—that between you and the women the play may please.
If I were a woman, I would kiss as many of you as had
beards that pleased me, complexions that liked me, and
breaths that I defied not. And I am sure as many as have
good beards, or good faces, or sweet breaths will, for
my kind offer, when I make curtsy, bid me farewell.
